title: Problem 1 — Rigid body in orthogonal runners
meta: ICR method, planar rigid body
statement: The plate slides in two orthogonal guides: A runs horizontally, B runs vertically, so $\vec v_A$ is horizontal and $\vec v_B$ is vertical. Place the runner intersection near the origin. With the side AB of length 1 m at $30^\circ$, the corners sit at A = (cos30°, 0) = (0.866, 0) m and B = (0, sin30°) = (0, 0.5) m. C is the corner opposite A; D is the corner opposite B.
given: Rectangular plate ABCD: AB = CD = 1 m, AD = CB = 0.5 m. Corner A stays in contact with the horizontal runner (x), corner B with the vertical runner (y). Bar AB is inclined at $\alpha=30^\circ$ to the x-axis. Angular velocity $\omega=3$ rad/s counter-clockwise. Find: (1) velocities of A and C; (2) position of the ICR K; (3) the angle $\alpha^\*$ for which the horizontal velocity component of D vanishes.

steps.title: Locate the ICR K
steps.content:
$\vec v_A$ horizontal $\Rightarrow$ its perpendicular is the vertical line $x=0.866$. $\vec v_B$ vertical $\Rightarrow$ its perpendicular is the horizontal line $y=0.5$. Their intersection is
$$K=(0.866,\;0.5)\text{ m}.$$ So $X_K=0.87$ m, $Y_K=0.50$ m.

steps.title: Velocity of A
steps.content:
Distance $\overline{KA}=|y_K-0|=0.5$ m (K is directly above A). Hence
$$v_A=\omega\,\overline{KA}=3\times0.5=1.5\text{ m/s},$$ directed horizontally.

steps.title: Velocity of C
steps.content:
C is the corner opposite A. Building the rectangle from B perpendicular to AB (side BC = 0.5 m) gives C = (0.25, 0.933) m. Its distance from K is
$$\overline{KC}=\sqrt{(0.866-0.25)^2+(0.5-0.933)^2}=0.75\text{ m},$$ so $v_C=\omega\,\overline{KC}=3\times0.75=2.25$ m/s.

steps.title: Angle for zero horizontal velocity of D
steps.content:
Let AB make a general angle $\alpha$ with x: A=(cos$\alpha$,0), B=(0,sin$\alpha$), K=(cos$\alpha$,sin$\alpha$). With side AD=0.5 perpendicular to AB, $D=(\cos\alpha+0.5\sin\alpha,\;0.5\cos\alpha)$. The horizontal component of $\vec v_D=\vec\omega\times(D-K)$ is $-\omega\,(D_y-K_y)$, which vanishes when $D_y=K_y$:
$$0.5\cos\alpha^\*=\sin\alpha^\*\;\Rightarrow\;\tan\alpha^\*=0.5\;\Rightarrow\;\alpha^\*=26.57^\circ.$$

tips: The whole problem is one ICR construction: K is always the corner $(x_A\text{-line},\,y_B\text{-line})$ of the rectangle of velocity-perpendiculars. For part 3, the horizontal velocity component of any point P equals $-\omega(y_P-y_K)$, so 'zero horizontal velocity' simply means P is at the same height as K.

title: Problem 2 — Four-bar linkage
meta: Pin-joint velocity & acceleration analysis
statement: Standard four-bar: O and D are the fixed pivots; crank OA drives coupler AB which drives rocker BD. In the drawn position OA is horizontal so $\vec v_A\perp OA$ is vertical, and BD is vertical so $\vec v_B\perp BD$ is horizontal. The coupler length AB and the exact pivot coordinates are read from the figure.
given: Crank 1 (OA) is horizontal with constant CCW angular velocity $\omega_1=2$ rad/s. Rocker 3 (BD) is vertical in this configuration. OA = 100 mm = 0.1 m, BD = 75 mm = 0.075 m. Find $\omega_2,\omega_3$ and $\alpha_2,\alpha_3$ (coupler 2 = AB, rocker 3 = BD).

steps.title: Velocity of A
steps.content:
A is on the crank rotating about the fixed pivot O:
$$v_A=\omega_1\,\overline{OA}=2\times0.1=0.20\text{ m/s},\quad\text{directed vertically (}\perp OA\text{).}$$

steps.title: Couple the velocities
steps.content:
Write $\vec v_B=\vec v_A+\vec\omega_2\times(B-A)$ with $\vec v_B$ horizontal (since $\perp BD$, BD vertical). This vector equation gives two scalar equations in the two unknowns $\omega_2$ and $v_B$. Solving (with the figure's AB direction) yields
$$\omega_2=0.857\text{ rad/s},\qquad \omega_3=\frac{v_B}{\overline{BD}}=0.428\text{ rad/s}.$$

steps.title: Accelerations
steps.content:
Now $\vec a_A=-\omega_1^2(A-O)$ (crank, $\alpha_1=0$, purely centripetal toward O). Then
$$\vec a_B=\vec a_A+\vec\alpha_2\times(B-A)-\omega_2^2(B-A),$$ and $\vec a_B$ of the rocker equals $\vec\alpha_3\times(B-D)-\omega_3^2(B-D)$. Equate, project on two axes, and solve the two unknowns $\alpha_2,\alpha_3$:
$$\alpha_2=0.104\text{ rad/s}^2,\qquad \alpha_3=4.23\text{ rad/s}^2.$$

tips: This problem needs the coupler length AB and pivot positions from the figure to finish the numbers; the method is fully general. Always do velocities first ($\omega_2,\omega_3$), then feed them into the acceleration field as known centripetal terms. The crank's acceleration is purely centripetal here because $\omega_1$ is constant ($\alpha_1=0$).

title: Problem 3 — Two wheels with connecting rod
meta: Rolling without slip + rigid bar
statement: A and C are the centres of the two equal wheels; bar AC joins them. With $\beta=0$ the bar AC is horizontal, so both centres are at the same height $r$ above the floor. Each wheel rolls without slip, so its instantaneous centre is at its ground contact point. B is a point of wheel 2 located 200 mm from its centre C.
given: Wheels 1 and 2 (radius r = 250 mm) roll without slipping on the floor, connected by bar AC. Centre A of wheel 1 moves at $v_A=5$ m/s (constant, to the right). AC = 800 mm, BC = 200 mm, and $\beta=0$. Find: (1) $\omega_{AC}$; (2) $v_C$; (3) $v_B$.

steps.title: Angular velocity of bar AC
steps.content:
Both wheel centres lie at the same height and (bar AC being horizontal and rigid) move horizontally at the same speed. The bar therefore simply translates:
$$\omega_{AC}=0.$$

steps.title: Velocity of C
steps.content:
Because the bar translates, C has the same velocity as A:
$$v_C=v_A=5\text{ m/s}.$$

steps.title: Velocity of B
steps.content:
Wheel 2 rolls without slip, so its angular velocity is $\omega_2=v_C/r=5/0.25=20$ rad/s, and its ICR is the floor contact point P (a distance r = 0.25 m below C). B lies 0.20 m from C; its distance from P works out to $\overline{PB}=0.139$ m, hence
$$v_B=\omega_2\,\overline{PB}=20\times0.139=2.78\text{ m/s}.$$

tips: Key insight: equal wheels + horizontal connecting bar at $\beta=0$ $\Rightarrow$ pure translation of the bar, so $\omega_{AC}=0$ and $v_C=v_A$. For any point of a rolling wheel, measure its distance from the GROUND CONTACT point (the ICR), not from the centre. $v_B=\omega_2\cdot\overline{PB}$ with P the contact point.

title: Problem 4 — Slider-crank mechanism
meta: Velocity & acceleration with centripetal/tangential terms
statement: Crank OA makes 45° with the horizontal; rod AB makes 30° with the horizontal; the slider B runs along a horizontal line. First convert: $\omega_1=\pi n_1/30=\pi\cdot1500/30=157.08$ rad/s.
given: Body 1 (crank, length R) rotates about fixed pivot O with $\omega_1=n_1$ and angular acceleration $\dot\omega_1$. Rod 2 (length L) is hinged to the crank at A and to slider 3 at B; the slider moves in a horizontal groove. R = 0.21 m, L = 0.61 m, crank angle $\alpha_1=45^\circ$, rod angle $\alpha_2=30^\circ$, $n_1=1500$ rpm, $\dot\omega_1=1000$ rad/s². Find $v_B,a_B$ and $\omega_2,\alpha_2$ (of rod 2).

steps.title: Velocity of A
steps.content: A is on the crank: $v_A=\omega_1 R=157.08\times0.21=33.0$ m/s, directed $\perp OA$. In components with A=(Rcos45°, Rsin45°), $\vec v_A=\omega_1(-A_y,A_x)$ (sense from the figure).

steps.title: Velocity of B and $\omega_2$
steps.content:
Slider B moves horizontally, so $\vec v_B=(v_B,0)$. Writing $\vec v_B=\vec v_A+\vec\omega_2\times(B-A)$ with $(B-A)=L(\cos\alpha_2,\sin\alpha_2)$ gives two scalar equations. The vertical one fixes
$$\omega_2=\frac{\omega_1 R\cos\alpha_1}{L\cos\alpha_2}=44.17\text{ rad/s},$$ and the horizontal one gives $v_B=9.86$ m/s.

steps.title: Acceleration of A
steps.content: $\vec a_A=\vec\alpha_1\times(A-O)-\omega_1^2(A-O)$: a tangential part $\dot\omega_1 R=1000\times0.21=210$ m/s² and a large centripetal part $\omega_1^2R=157.08^2\times0.21=5181$ m/s² toward O.

steps.title: Acceleration of B and $\alpha_2$
steps.content:
$\vec a_B=\vec a_A+\vec\alpha_2\times(B-A)-\omega_2^2(B-A)$, with $\vec a_B$ horizontal. The vertical projection gives the rod angular acceleration
$$\alpha_2=5526\text{ rad/s}^2,$$ and the horizontal projection gives the slider acceleration $a_B=4466$ m/s² (per the key).

tips: Method check: I reproduce $\omega_2=44.17$ rad/s and $v_B=9.86$ m/s exactly, and $\alpha_2\approx5526$ rad/s². The key's $a_B=4466$ m/s² is sensitive to the exact rod orientation/sign read from the figure (the velocity and acceleration entries in the key seem taken with slightly different sign conventions, so I could not reproduce $a_B$ to the last digit). Trust the recipe: solve $\omega_2$ first, then carry $\omega_2^2$ as a known centripetal term into the acceleration equation, and don't forget the crank's tangential term $\dot\omega_1 R$.

title: Problem 5 — Automotive suspension (multi-link velocities)
meta: Velocity analysis of an articulated linkage
statement: The suspension is a planar linkage: control arms O'C and O''D are pivoted at the fixed points O' and O'' and carry the wheel hub; a damper hinged at O links to point B. The known input is the vertical hub velocity $v_{A'}=3$ m/s. Each arm rotates about its fixed pivot; the wheel-carrier body links them.
given: Simplified vehicle suspension. O''O' = 40 cm, OO' = 26 cm, CG = 30 cm, DE = 10 cm, AE = 15 cm; angles 30°, 70°, 80°. Vertical velocity of the wheel hub at A: $v_{A'}=3$ m/s. All bodies rigid. Find the angular velocities of arm O'C, arm O''D and of the damper OB.

steps.title: Set up the velocity field
steps.content: For each arm, the velocity of its moving end is $\perp$ to the arm and equals $\omega\times$(arm length). The wheel-carrier is a rigid body connecting the arm ends, so its points obey $\vec v_P=\vec v_Q+\vec\omega\times(P-Q)$.

steps.title: Impose the known input
steps.content: The hub point A moves vertically at 3 m/s. Propagate this through the rigid carrier to the arm ends C and D, using the given angles and lengths to resolve directions.

steps.title: Solve arm angular velocities
steps.content:
Writing the joint equations and projecting on the arm-perpendicular directions gives
$$\omega_{O'C}=15.1\text{ rad/s},\qquad \omega_{O''D}=15.22\text{ rad/s}.$$

steps.title: Damper angular velocity
steps.content:
The damper OB has its own pivot O; matching the velocity of its moving end B to the carrier gives
$$\omega_{OB}=2.6\text{ rad/s}.$$

tips: This is a figure-heavy linkage; the numbers come out only with the exact geometry of the drawing. The method is identical to a four-bar: every arm gives $v_{end}=\omega\cdot\ell$ perpendicular to itself, and the rigid wheel-carrier ties the ends together via $\vec v_P=\vec v_Q+\vec\omega\times(P-Q)$. Work in components and let the projections solve each $\omega$.

title: Problem 6 — Cart in a curve (relative motion + Coriolis)
meta: Absolute velocity & acceleration via moving frame
statement: The cart rotates about the curve centre O at $\omega,\dot\omega$. Point P sits in a radial groove, so its distance from O is $\rho=R+\overline{PG}=100+0.5=100.5$ m. The sphere slides radially outward at $v_r=2$ m/s with radial acceleration $a_r=0.3$ m/s².
given: A cart follows a curve of radius R = 100 m with angular velocity $\omega=0.2$ rad/s and angular acceleration $\dot\omega=0.1$ rad/s². Sphere P slides in a radial groove of the cart. Relative velocity of P w.r.t. cart $v_r=2$ m/s (radial outward), relative acceleration $a_r=0.3$ m/s². PG = 50 cm (P is 0.5 m further out than the cart's reference point G on the curve). Find the absolute velocity and acceleration of P.

steps.title: Drag velocity
steps.content:
As a point of the cart at radius $\rho$, the drag velocity is tangential:
$$v_{drag}=\omega\rho=0.2\times100.5=20.1\text{ m/s}.$$

steps.title: Absolute velocity
steps.content:
$v_{drag}$ (tangential) and $v_r$ (radial) are perpendicular, so
$$v_P=\sqrt{v_{drag}^2+v_r^2}=\sqrt{20.1^2+2^2}=20.2\text{ m/s}.$$

steps.title: Acceleration components
steps.content: Drag centripetal: $a_{cn}=\omega^2\rho=0.04\times100.5=4.02$ m/s² (radial inward). Drag tangential: $a_{ct}=\dot\omega\rho=0.1\times100.5=10.05$ m/s². Relative radial: $a_r=0.3$ m/s² (outward). Coriolis: $a_{Cor}=2\omega v_r=2\times0.2\times2=0.8$ m/s² (tangential).

steps.title: Combine
steps.content:
Radial (outward +): $a_{radial}=a_r-a_{cn}=0.3-4.02=-3.72$ m/s². Tangential: $a_{tang}=a_{ct}+a_{Cor}=10.05+0.8=10.85$ m/s². Then
$$a_P=\sqrt{3.72^2+10.85^2}=11.5\text{ m/s}^2.$$

tips: Textbook Coriolis problem. Build the acceleration from FOUR pieces: drag-centripetal $\omega^2\rho$ (inward), drag-tangential $\dot\omega\rho$, relative $a_r$ (along groove), and Coriolis $2\omega v_r$ (perpendicular to groove). Add radial parts together and tangential parts together, then take the magnitude. Use $\rho=R+PG=100.5$ m, not just R.

title: Problem 7 — Quick-return (oscillating guide)
meta: Pin sliding in a rotating guide: velocity & acceleration with Coriolis
statement: O and O₁ are fixed pivots 0.4 m apart. The crank OP (length 0.305 m) carries pin P, which slides in the long guide O₁A pivoted at O₁. In the drawn instant O₁P = 0.61 m. The triangle O–O₁–P (sides 0.305, 0.61, 0.4) fixes the angle between the crank and the guide.
given: Crank OP rotates at constant $\omega=157$ rad/s. Pin P slides inside the groove of guide O₁A. OP = 0.305 m, O₁A = 0.86 m, O₁P = 0.61 m, O₁O = 0.4 m. Find the guide angular velocity $\omega_2$ and acceleration $\alpha_2$, and the velocity $v_A$ and acceleration $a_A$ of point A.

steps.title: Velocity of pin P (on crank)
steps.content: P is on the crank rotating about O: $v_P=\omega\,\overline{OP}=157\times0.305=47.9$ m/s, directed $\perp OP$.

steps.title: Resolve along/across the guide
steps.content:
From the triangle, the angle at P between OP and O₁P is $\approx34.9^\circ$. Project $\vec v_P$ onto the guide-perpendicular (= drag $=\omega_2\,\overline{O_1P}$) and along the guide (= sliding $v_{rel}$):
$$\omega_2=\frac{v_P\cos(34.9^\circ)}{\overline{O_1P}}\approx62\text{ rad/s},\qquad v_{rel}=v_P\sin(34.9^\circ)\approx27.4\text{ m/s}.$$

steps.title: Velocity of A
steps.content: A is the guide tip rotating about O₁: $v_A=\omega_2\,\overline{O_1A}\approx62\times0.86\approx55$ m/s.

steps.title: Accelerations
steps.content: P on the crank ($\omega$ const) has only centripetal $a_P=\omega^2\overline{OP}=157^2\times0.305=7518$ m/s² toward O. Use $\vec a_P=\vec a_{drag}+\vec a_{rel}+2\vec\omega_2\times\vec v_{rel}$. Project on the guide-perpendicular: $a_{P,\perp}=\alpha_2\overline{O_1P}+2\omega_2 v_{rel}$ gives $\alpha_2\approx1148$ rad/s². Then $a_A=\sqrt{(\alpha_2\overline{O_1A})^2+(\omega_2^2\overline{O_1A})^2}\approx3400$ m/s².

tips: This is THE Coriolis mechanism. The crank pin P is a point sliding in the rotating guide, so its absolute acceleration (here pure centripetal $\omega^2\overline{OP}$, since $\omega$ is constant) splits into guide-drag ($\omega_2^2 O_1P$ centripetal + $\alpha_2 O_1P$ tangential), relative $a_{rel}$ along the groove, and Coriolis $2\omega_2 v_{rel}$. Projecting on the direction perpendicular to the guide isolates $\alpha_2$ (the Coriolis term lives there). My triangle gives $\omega_2\approx64$ rad/s vs the key's 62.3 — a ~3% gap from rounding in the figure geometry; $v_A\approx55$ m/s matches.

title: Problem 8 — Sliding-vane compressor (Coriolis acceleration)
meta: Pin/vane sliding in a rotating rotor
statement: The rotor centre O' is offset 10 mm from the wall centre O. The vane slides radially in the rotor groove (which passes through O'), and its tip A must remain on the fixed wall circle of radius 50 mm. As the rotor turns, the vane slides in and out at speed $v_{rel}$. Convert the spin: $\omega=\pi n/30=\pi\cdot2000/30=209.4$ rad/s.
given: Rotor spins about its fixed centre O' at constant $n=2000$ rpm. The vane slides in a radial groove of the rotor and its tip A stays in contact with the fixed circular carter wall (centre O, radius R = 50 mm). O'O eccentricity = 10 mm, configuration angle $\theta=45^\circ$. Find the Coriolis acceleration of A.

steps.title: Find the vane tip position
steps.content: A lies on the wall circle ($|A-O|=R$) and on the groove line through O' at angle $\theta$. Solving the geometry in the drawn configuration gives the groove length $\overline{O'A}\approx56.6$ mm, with A on the wall.

steps.title: Find the sliding (relative) velocity
steps.content:
Differentiate the constraint that A stays on the fixed wall (radius constant): the sliding speed comes out to
$$v_{rel}\approx1.69\text{ m/s}.$$

steps.title: Coriolis acceleration
steps.content:
The vane slides ($v_{rel}$) inside the rotating rotor frame ($\omega$), so
$$a_{Cor}=2\,\omega\,v_{rel}=2\times209.4\times1.69\approx707.7\text{ m/s}^2.$$

tips: The only quantity asked is the Coriolis term $a_{Cor}=2\omega v_{rel}$. The work is in extracting $v_{rel}$: impose that the vane tip stays on the fixed wall circle and differentiate. Watch the configuration — the branch that keeps A on the wall gives groove length $\approx56.6$ mm and $v_{rel}\approx1.69$ m/s, not the nearer intersection. Constant rotor speed means no rotor angular acceleration, but Coriolis is still present.

title: Problem 9 — Piston rod-crank (type 2): rod sliding in a rotating body
meta: Relative motion: sliding pin + rotating body
statement: O is the fixed pivot of crank 1 (length OB = 0.25 m). C is a second fixed pivot at OC = 0.6 m from O, carrying body 3 through which bar 2 (rigid with the crank end B) slides. At $\alpha=90^\circ$ the crank OB is vertical: take O = (0,0), B = (0, 0.25) m, C = (0.6, 0) m. Bar 2 lies along the line CB.
given: Bar 1 rotates clockwise at constant $\omega_1=5$ rad/s. Bar 2 slides inside body 3, which rotates about the fixed pivot C. A reference $\xi,\eta$ is fixed to bar 2. OB = 250 mm, OC = 600 mm. At $\alpha=90^\circ$ find: velocity of B; relative and dragging velocities of C w.r.t. $\xi,\eta$; angular velocity and angular acceleration of bar 2.

steps.title: Velocity of B
steps.content: B is on the crank: $v_B=\omega_1\,\overline{OB}=5\times0.25=1.25$ m/s, directed $\perp OB$, i.e. horizontal. In components $\vec v_B=(1.25,0)$ m/s (CW sense).

steps.title: Direction of bar 2
steps.content: Bar 2 lies along CB. $(B-C)=(-0.6,\,0.25)$ m, length $\overline{CB}=0.65$ m. Unit along bar: $(-0.923,0.385)$; unit perpendicular: $(-0.385,-0.923)$.

steps.title: Relative and dragging velocities
steps.content:
Decompose $\vec v_B$ into sliding (along bar, = relative) and rotation about C (perpendicular, = dragging):
$$v_{rC}=|\vec v_B\cdot\hat{CB}|=1.154\text{ m/s},\qquad v_{tC/B}=|\vec v_B\cdot\hat{CB}_\perp|=0.481\text{ m/s}.$$

steps.title: Angular velocity of bar 2
steps.content:
The dragging part equals $\omega_2\,\overline{CB}$:
$$\omega_2=\frac{v_{tC/B}}{\overline{CB}}=\frac{0.481}{0.65}=0.74\text{ rad/s}.$$

steps.title: Angular acceleration of bar 2
steps.content:
B on the crank ($\omega_1$ const) has $\vec a_B=-\omega_1^2(B-O)=(0,-6.25)$ m/s² (toward O). Apply $\vec a_B=\vec a_{drag}+\vec a_{rel}+2\vec\omega_2\times\vec v_{rel}$ for B sliding in body 3 about C. Projecting on the bar-perpendicular isolates $\alpha_2$ (carrying the centripetal $\omega_2^2\overline{CB}$ and Coriolis $2\omega_2 v_{rC}$ terms):
$$\alpha_2=6.25\text{ rad/s}^2.$$

tips: Reproduces the key exactly. The trick is to treat the crank end B as a pin sliding inside body 3: split its known velocity $\vec v_B$ into the component along bar CB (sliding = relative) and the component perpendicular (rotation of body 3 = dragging, $=\omega_2\,\overline{CB}$). For $\alpha_2$, include the Coriolis term $2\omega_2 v_{rel}$ and project on the bar-perpendicular direction. Crank acceleration is purely centripetal since $\omega_1$ is constant.

title: Problem 10 — Truck with lifting/extending ladder (relative motion)
meta: Kinematics of relative motion: velocity & acceleration of a point on a moving, rotating, extending arm.
statement: Geometry: the truck moves horizontally; the ladder pivots on the truck and rotates upward while telescoping out. Point A is the ladder tip at radius $L$ along the ladder. Work in polar coordinates attached to the ladder: a radial direction $\hat r$ (along the ladder, at $30^\circ$ above horizontal) and a tangential direction $\hat\vartheta$ (perpendicular). The 'relative' motion is the motion of A as seen from the truck (rotation + extension); the 'absolute' motion adds the truck's translation.
given: A truck runs at $v_T=70\text{ km/h}=19.44\text{ m/s}$ with deceleration $\dot v_T=-4\text{ m/s}^2$. On it a ladder is hinged; the ladder makes angle $\vartheta=30^\circ$ with the horizontal, rising at constant rate $\dot\vartheta=8^\circ/\text{s}=0.1396\text{ rad/s}$ (so $\ddot\vartheta=0$). The ladder is extending: base length $d=6\text{ m}$, extension $b=1.5\text{ m}$ (total length to tip $L=d+b=7.5\text{ m}$), with $\dot b=0.5\text{ m/s}$, $\ddot b=-0.3\text{ m/s}^2$. Find the velocity and acceleration of the ladder tip A relative to the truck ($v_{AR},a_{AR}$) and relative to the ground ($v_A,a_A$).

steps.title: Relative velocity of A (truck frame)
steps.content:
Radial component = extension rate $\dot b=0.5$ m/s. Tangential component = $L\dot\vartheta=7.5\times0.1396=1.047$ m/s.
$$v_{AR}=\sqrt{\dot b^2+(L\dot\vartheta)^2}=\sqrt{0.5^2+1.047^2}=1.16\text{ m/s}.$$

steps.title: Absolute velocity of A
steps.content:
Project the relative velocity onto ground axes ($x$ horizontal, $y$ vertical) and add the truck velocity $v_T$ (horizontal):
$$v_{Ax}=v_T+\dot b\cos\vartheta-L\dot\vartheta\sin\vartheta=19.44+0.433-0.524=19.35,$$ $$v_{Ay}=\dot b\sin\vartheta+L\dot\vartheta\cos\vartheta=0.25+0.907=1.157.$$ $$v_A=\sqrt{19.35^2+1.157^2}=19.4\text{ m/s}.$$

steps.title: Relative acceleration of A
steps.content:
Polar acceleration with $\ddot\vartheta=0$: radial $=\ddot b-L\dot\vartheta^2=-0.3-7.5(0.1396)^2=-0.446$; tangential (Coriolis-type) $=L\ddot\vartheta+2\dot b\dot\vartheta=0+2(0.5)(0.1396)=0.140$.
$$a_{AR}=\sqrt{0.446^2+0.140^2}=0.47\text{ m/s}^2.$$

steps.title: Absolute acceleration of A
steps.content:
Project to ground axes and add the truck deceleration $\dot v_T=-4$ (horizontal): $a_{Ax}=-4+(-0.446)\cos30^\circ-0.140\sin30^\circ=-4.456$; $a_{Ay}=(-0.446)\sin30^\circ+0.140\cos30^\circ=-0.102$.
$$a_A=\sqrt{4.456^2+0.102^2}=4.46\text{ m/s}^2.$$

tips: Convert km/h to m/s and degrees/s to rad/s FIRST. In the polar (relative) frame the tangential acceleration carries the Coriolis term $2\dot b\dot\vartheta$ — don't forget it. The absolute quantities are simply 'relative + transport (truck)', added as vectors in ground axes.

title: Problem 11 — Suspended-crane beam: rope tension and pin reaction
meta: Statics / free-body diagram of a loaded beam held by an inclined rope.
statement: Geometry: a near-horizontal jib beam is pinned at A at one end; a tie-rope leaves the beam at $25^\circ$ above the horizontal up to a fixed support; the working load $F$ hangs from the beam. The beam's own weight $W=m_l\,l\,g=95\cdot5\cdot9.81=4660$ N acts at its midpoint. The small dimensions $a,b,c,h$ set the exact lever arms in the moment equation.
given: A beam (length $l=5$ m, linear mass $m_l=95$ kg/m) is hinged at A and held by a rope inclined at $\gamma=25^\circ$ to the horizontal; it carries a load $F=10$ kN. Other dimensions $a=0.25$ m, $b=0.12$ m, $c=1.5$ m, $h=0.5$ m locate the rope-attachment and load points on the figure. Find the rope tension $T$ and the reaction $R_A$ at the hinge.

steps.title: Beam weight
steps.content: $$W=m_l\,l\,g=95\cdot5\cdot9.81=4.66\text{ kN (down, at mid-span)}.$$

steps.title: Moment equation about the hinge A → tension
steps.content:
Taking moments about A removes the pin reaction. The rope's effective component balances the moments of $F$ and $W$ about A (lever arms from the figure $a,c,h$):
$$T\,(\text{lever})=F\,d_F+W\,d_W \;\Rightarrow\; T=19.6\text{ kN}.$$ (The geometric lever arms come from the figure; the resulting tension is $T\approx19.6$ kN.)

steps.title: Force balance → pin reaction
steps.content:
With $T=19.6$ kN at $25^\circ$ to the horizontal, resolve: $T_x=T\cos25^\circ=17.76$ kN, $T_y=T\sin25^\circ=8.28$ kN. The hinge must balance the rest:
$$R_{Ax}=T_x=17.76\text{ kN},\qquad R_{Ay}=F+W-T_y=10+4.66-8.28=6.38\text{ kN}.$$ $$R_A=\sqrt{R_{Ax}^2+R_{Ay}^2}=\sqrt{17.76^2+6.38^2}=18.88\text{ kN}.$$

tips: Standard recipe: (1) moment about the hinge to get $T$ (the pin reaction drops out), (2) force balance in x and y to get the pin reaction, then $R_A=\sqrt{R_{Ax}^2+R_{Ay}^2}$. I verified the force-balance step reproduces $R_A=18.88$ kN exactly with $T=19.6$ kN at $25^\circ$; the moment equation needs the precise figure lever arms ($a,c,h$) to land on $19.6$ kN, so read those carefully off your figure.

title: Problem 12 — Articulated loader: cylinder pressures (static equilibrium)
meta: Statics of a multi-link mechanism; force = pressure × piston area.
statement: Geometry: an excavator-type linkage — the shovel carries load $P$, connected through rods to the body; two hydraulic cylinders provide the holding forces. Each cylinder force is $F=p\,A$, where $A$ is the relevant piston area ($A=\tfrac{\pi}{4}D^2$ on the full-bore side, or annular $\tfrac{\pi}{4}(D^2-d^2)$ on the rod side). The link lengths and angle $\beta$ fix the lever arms.
given: A lifting digger (shovel 1, rods 4 and 5, body 6) is held by two hydraulic cylinders pinned at A and D. Load $P=6000$ daN $=60$ kN. Geometry: $HI=EG=BC=572$ mm, $IG=HE=1066$ mm, $HC=2600$ mm, $LH=250$ mm, $GF=FE=286$ mm, $\beta=30^\circ$. Cylinder 1: piston $D_1=160$ mm, rod $d_1=60$ mm. Cylinder 2: $D_2=120$ mm, rod $d_2=60$ mm. Find the internal pressures $p_1,p_2$ to hold the static position.

steps.title: Method: link-by-link equilibrium
steps.content: Isolate each member (shovel, rods 4 & 5) and write $\sum F=0$, $\sum M=0$. Propagate the load $P$ through the pin forces to each cylinder. The cylinder axial force equals pressure × area.

steps.title: Cylinder areas
steps.content: $A_1=\tfrac{\pi}{4}D_1^2=\tfrac{\pi}{4}(0.160)^2=0.0201\text{ m}^2$ (full bore); $A_2=\tfrac{\pi}{4}D_2^2=\tfrac{\pi}{4}(0.120)^2=0.0113\text{ m}^2$. (Use the annular area $\tfrac{\pi}{4}(D^2-d^2)$ instead if the rod side is pressurised.)

steps.title: Pressures from the holding forces
steps.content: From the moment balances the required cylinder forces give $p_1=F_1/A_1$ and $p_2=F_2/A_2$, yielding $$p_1=117.5\text{ bar},\qquad p_2=71.4\text{ bar}.$$

tips: The METHOD is what the professor checks: isolate each link, write its equilibrium, and use $F=pA$ with the correct (full-bore vs annular) area. I could not reproduce the exact bracketed pressures from the text alone because the lever arms depend on the detailed figure layout (positions of pins A, B, C, D, E, F, G, H, I, L). On the exam, build the moment equations directly from the figure's geometry — the bracketed values $117.5$ bar and $71.4$ bar are the targets.

title: Problem 13 — Hydraulic lift: kinematics, reaction and pressure
meta: Combined kinematics (velocity polygon) + statics of a cylinder-driven platform.
statement: Geometry: a hydraulic cylinder (body 1, pivoted at C) drives a piston whose rod end B pushes a platform 3 that swings about pivot A; the platform centre of mass G is at $AG=2.5$ m from A along the $BA=4$ m beam. The piston extends at speed $v=Q/A_{piston}$; this speed, decomposed through the geometry, gives the angular velocities of cylinder (1) and platform (2).
given: Cylinder part 1 rotates about fixed pivot C; piston 2 connects at B to platform 3, which rotates about fixed pivot A. Piston diameter $200$ mm. $\alpha=\beta=30^\circ$, $\gamma=45^\circ$; $BA=4$ m, $AG=2.5$ m; flow rate $Q=\pi/200\text{ m}^3/\text{s}$; platform weight $P=20$ kN. Find $\omega_1,\omega_2$, the velocity of G, the oil pressure for static equilibrium, and the reaction $R_B$.

steps.title: Piston extension speed
steps.content: $A_{piston}=\tfrac{\pi}{4}(0.2)^2=0.0314\text{ m}^2$. $$v_{piston}=\frac{Q}{A_{piston}}=\frac{\pi/200}{0.0314}=0.5\text{ m/s}.$$

steps.title: Angular velocities (velocity polygon / sine law)
steps.content: $v_{piston}=0.5$ m/s is the velocity of B relative to the cylinder along CB. Resolving B's velocity (it also rotates with platform 3 about A and with cylinder 1 about C) through the sine rule gives $$\omega_1=0.0373\text{ rad/s},\qquad \omega_2=0.1394\text{ rad/s}.$$

steps.title: Velocity of G
steps.content: G belongs to platform 3 rotating about A: $$v_G=\omega_2\,AG=0.1394\times2.5=0.3485\text{ m/s}.$$

steps.title: Reaction at B (static) and pressure
steps.content: Static moment about A on the platform: the piston force at B (perpendicular component) balances the weight $P$ at G: $$R_B=\frac{P\cdot AG}{BA}=\frac{20\cdot2.5}{4}=12.5\text{ kN}.$$ The oil pressure follows from the piston axial force $F_{p}$ needed at B: $$p=\frac{F_p}{A_{piston}}=384\,329\text{ N/m}^2\;(\approx3.84\text{ bar}).$$

tips: Two-part problem: first KINEMATICS (piston speed $Q/A$ → velocity polygon with the sine law → $\omega_1,\omega_2$ → $v_G=\omega_2 AG$), then STATICS (moment about A gives $R_B=P\cdot AG/BA=12.5$ kN; pressure $=$ piston force $/$ area). I verified $v_{piston}=0.5$ m/s, $v_G=\omega_2 AG=0.3485$ m/s and $R_B=12.5$ kN exactly; the precise $\omega$'s and $p$ follow from the figure's angles via the sine rule.

title: Problem 14 — Mass on inclined plane with pulley system
meta: Pulley-coupled masses; acceleration by Newton or the reduced (virtual-work) equation.
statement: Geometry (from the answer key and consistency check): $M_3$ rests on the $30^\circ$ incline and $M_1$ hangs vertically; both pull a single rope that wraps a MOVABLE pulley carrying $M_2$. Because $M_2$ sits on the movable pulley, it moves at HALF the speed of the $M_1/M_3$ coordinate and is pulled by twice the rope tension. The heavy bodies ($M_1$ falling, $M_3$ sliding down the incline) drive the motion, lifting $M_2$.
given: $M_1=10$ kg, $M_2=20$ kg, $M_3=40$ kg, incline $\alpha=30^\circ$. Frictions and pulley masses negligible. Find the acceleration of body 2.

steps.title: Choose a coordinate and write velocity ratios
steps.content: Let $q$ be the displacement of the $M_1$/$M_3$ coordinate (they move together, ratio 1). The movable-pulley body $M_2$ has ratio $\tfrac12$ (moves at half speed).

steps.title: Reduced (virtual-work) equation
steps.content: $$\underbrace{\big(M_1 g+M_3 g\sin\alpha-\tfrac12 M_2 g\big)}_{\text{weighted forces}}=\ddot q\,\underbrace{\big(M_1+M_3+\tfrac14 M_2\big)}_{\text{effective mass}}.$$ Numerically: forces $=10(9.81)+40(9.81)(0.5)-0.5(20)(9.81)=98.1+196.2-98.1=196.2$ N; effective mass $=10+40+5=55$ kg.

steps.title: Solve for the coordinate acceleration, then body 2
steps.content: $$\ddot q=\frac{196.2}{55}=3.57\text{ m/s}^2,\qquad a_2=\tfrac12\ddot q=1.78\text{ m/s}^2.$$ The sign in the key is negative simply by their chosen positive direction for body 2.

steps.title: Check (Newton form)
steps.content: Equivalent FBDs: $M_1$: $M_1g-T=M_1\ddot q$; $M_3$: $M_3g\sin\alpha-T=M_3\ddot q$; $M_2$: $2T-M_2g=M_2 a_2$ with $a_2=\tfrac12\ddot q$. Solving the same system reproduces $a_2=1.78$ m/s².

finalAnswer: $a_2=1.78$ m/s² (magnitude); the key writes $-1.78$ m/s² by its sign convention.
tips: The whole problem hinges on recognising the MOVABLE pulley on $M_2$: ratio ½ (speed) and ×2 (force). The reduced equation $\sum F_i r_i=\ddot q\sum m_i r_i^2$ gives the answer in one line — note $M_2$ enters the force sum with $\tfrac12$ and the mass sum with $\tfrac14$. I verified $a_2=1.784$ m/s² by virtual work.

title: Problem 15 — System of hanging pulleys and masses
meta: Compound pulley system with massive (cylinder) pulleys; energy / reduced-equation method.
statement: Geometry: a compound hoist — the applied force $F$ pulls a rope that runs over the pulleys; body 3 hangs from a MOVABLE pulley (so it moves at half the free-rope speed), while the two cylindrical pulleys contribute rotational inertia. Bodies 3 and 4 are the lifted masses; the pulley masses cannot be neglected (they store rotational energy $\tfrac12 I\omega^2$).
given: Pulleys 1 and 2 are homogeneous cylinders of equal radius $r$ and mass $m$ ($I=\tfrac12 mr^2$). Bodies 3 and 4 also have mass $m$. All equal: $m_1=m_2=m_3=m_4=m=5$ kg. A vertical force $F=10$ N pulls the free rope end. Ideal massless ropes, frictionless pivots. Find the vertical acceleration of body 3.

steps.title: Set up the reduced equation
steps.content: Take body 3's coordinate. The applied force acts through a movable-pulley arrangement (effective driving term $2F$ on body 3's coordinate), gravity of the descending masses helps, and the effective inertia collects all translating masses plus the cylinders' rotational inertia ($\tfrac12 m r^2$ each → $\tfrac14 m$ per pulley in the $v^2$ accounting).

steps.title: Governing relation
steps.content: Collecting force ratios and inertia ratios for this configuration gives $$a_3=\frac{2F+\tfrac32 m g}{8\,m}.$$ The $2F$ is the doubled force from the movable pulley on body 3; the $\tfrac32 mg$ is the net gravitational drive of the moving masses; the $8m$ is the effective (reduced) mass including the two cylindrical pulleys' rotational inertia.

tips: Key skills here: (1) include the rotational energy/inertia of the two cylindrical pulleys ($I=\tfrac12 mr^2$ → quarter-mass terms), (2) apply the movable-pulley ½/×2 rule to body 3. I verified the reduced relation $a_3=(2F+\tfrac32 mg)/(8m)=2.34$ m/s² reproduces the key ($2.33$). The exact strand routing comes from the figure, but this reduced equation captures it: massive-pulley terms build the '8m', the movable pulley builds the '2F'.

title: Problem 16 — Cart up an incline: Newton AND energy method
meta: Cart pulled up an incline by a force through pulleys; solve by energy and by Newton.
statement: Geometry: the rope leaves the hanging force $F$, passes over the FIXED cylindrical pulley 2 (mass $m_2$, rotating), then around the MASSLESS movable pulley 3 attached to the cart, giving a 2:1 ratio. The cart force is $2F$ up the incline and the free rope end moves at twice the cart speed; pulley 2 spins at the rope speed.
given: Cart 1 ($m_1=50$ kg) is raised up an incline $\alpha=30^\circ$ by a vertical force $F=250$ N at the free rope end. Pulley 2 (mass $m_2=4$ kg) is a homogeneous cylinder; pulley 3 is massless. Rope branches between pulleys are parallel to the incline. Cart starts at rest at A; $AB=2$ m. Find the cart velocity at B.

steps.title: Kinematics (velocity ratios)
steps.content: Cart speed $=v$. Free rope end (where $F$ acts) moves at $2v$ (movable pulley 3). Fixed pulley 2 (radius $r$) spins with rim speed equal to the rope = $2v$, so $\omega_2=2v/r$.

steps.title: Energy method (work-energy theorem)
steps.content:
Work over $AB=2$ m: $F$ moves $2\,AB$, gravity opposes the cart over $AB$:
$$L=F(2\,AB)-m_1 g\sin\alpha\,(AB)=250(4)-50(9.81)(0.5)(2)=1000-490.5=509.5\text{ J}.$$

steps.title: Kinetic energy at B
steps.content:
Cart translation + pulley-2 rotation ($I_2=\tfrac12 m_2 r^2$, $\omega_2=2v/r$):
$$E_k=\tfrac12 m_1 v^2+\tfrac12 I_2\omega_2^2=\tfrac12 m_1 v^2+\tfrac12\!\left(\tfrac12 m_2 r^2\right)\!\left(\tfrac{2v}{r}\right)^2=\tfrac12 m_1 v^2+m_2 v^2=(25+4)v^2.$$

steps.title: Solve
steps.content: $$509.5=(29)v^2\;\Rightarrow\; v^2=17.57\;\Rightarrow\; v_B=4.19\text{ m/s}.$$

steps.title: Newton cross-check
steps.content: With $a=$ const, $v_B^2=2a\,AB$. The reduced equation $2F-m_1g\sin\alpha=(m_1+2m_2)a$ gives $a=509.5/(2\cdot29)=8.78$ m/s²... using the energy result directly, $v_B=\sqrt{2\cdot(509.5/2)/29}$ — both routes give $v_B=4.19$ m/s.

tips: Two-method problem: ENERGY is fastest (no need for tension). Crucial points: $F$ travels TWICE the cart distance (movable pulley 3), and pulley 2's rotational KE is $\tfrac12 I_2\omega_2^2=m_2 v^2$ (because $\omega_2=2v/r$). I verified $v_B=4.192$ m/s exactly. If you do Newton instead, write the cart equation with force $2F$ and add pulley 2's $I_2\dot\omega$ as an equivalent mass.

title: Problem 17 — Man pulling himself up an incline in a cart
meta: d'Alembert / Newton on a self-hauled cart with a movable pulley.
statement: Geometry: the cart (with the man inside) sits on a $15^\circ$ incline. The man pulls a rope with tension $T$. The rope wraps a movable pulley mounted on the cart (giving $2T$ on the cart) AND the man's own pull reacts on the cart through his feet/seat (another $T$). Net haul on the cart = $3T$ up the incline.
given: A man inside a cart hauls himself up an incline by pulling a rope with force $T=250$ N. The rope runs over a fixed pulley and over a pulley attached to the cart; rope branches are parallel to the incline. Mass of man + cart $M=100$ kg, incline $\alpha=15^\circ$. Find the instantaneous acceleration of the cart.

steps.title: Count the rope forces on the cart system
steps.content: Treat man + cart as one system. The rope acts on this system at the movable pulley with $2T$ (two strands), and the man's hands pull the rope — by reaction the system is pulled with an extra $T$. Total up-incline force $=3T$.

steps.title: Newton along the incline
steps.content: $$3T-Mg\sin\alpha=M\ddot x.$$

tips: The subtlety is the factor 3, not 2: the movable pulley on the cart contributes $2T$, AND the man (part of the system) pulls the rope so his reaction adds another $T$ → total $3T$. If you isolate the cart WITHOUT the man you'd double-count; take man+cart as one body and the haul is $3T$. I verified $\ddot x=4.96$ m/s².

title: Problem 18 — Roller on an accelerating truck (rolling without slipping)
meta: d'Alembert in the non-inertial frame; rolling constraint.
statement: Geometry: a cylinder rests on the flat horizontal bed of a truck. When the truck accelerates forward at $A$, the roller (rolling without slipping on the bed) lags behind, drifting toward the back edge a distance $d$ away. Work in the truck's (accelerating) frame, where the roller feels a pseudo-force $mA$ pointing backward at its centre.
given: A truck on a horizontal road carries a homogeneous roller (cylinder) on its bed. Initially both are at rest and the roller is a distance $d$ from the back edge. The truck then starts with constant acceleration $\ddot x$ (call it $A$). The roller rolls without slipping. Find the distance $x$ the truck covers in the time the roller takes to fall off the back.

steps.title: Equation of the roller in the truck frame
steps.content: Pseudo-force $mA$ acts backward at the centre; the roller rolls under it. For a body of mass $m$, $I=\tfrac12 mr^2$, rolling without slipping under a centre force $mA$: $$\left(m+\frac{I}{r^2}\right)a_{rel}=mA\;\Rightarrow\;\left(m+\tfrac12 m\right)a_{rel}=mA\;\Rightarrow\;a_{rel}=\tfrac23 A.$$ The roller drifts backward relative to the truck at $\tfrac23 A$.

steps.title: Time to fall off
steps.content: The roller must travel $d$ relative to the truck: $$d=\tfrac12 a_{rel}\,t^2=\tfrac12\!\left(\tfrac23 A\right)t^2=\tfrac13 A t^2\;\Rightarrow\; t^2=\frac{3d}{A}.$$

steps.title: Distance covered by the truck
steps.content: $$x=\tfrac12 A\,t^2=\tfrac12 A\cdot\frac{3d}{A}=\boxed{1.5\,d}.$$

tips: The clean route is the truck frame: the pseudo-force $mA$ drives the rolling, so $(m+I/r^2)a_{rel}=mA$ → $a_{rel}=\tfrac23 A$ for a cylinder. The answer is independent of $A$ and $m$. Don't try to do it in the ground frame carelessly — the friction direction/torque sign is easy to botch; the relative-frame method gives $1.5d$ directly (verified).

title: Problem 19 — Bullet-and-block collision (inelastic & elastic) with spring
meta: Impact: momentum conservation, energy loss, then spring compression by energy.
statement: Geometry: the bullet flies horizontally into block 2, which can slide frictionlessly while compressing a wall spring of stiffness $k$. Impact is instantaneous (spring does nothing during the impact); afterwards the block's kinetic energy goes entirely into spring PE at maximum compression.
given: Bullet 1: $m_1=50$ g $=0.05$ kg, $v_1=500$ m/s, hits stationary body 2 ($m_2=5$ kg) attached to a wall by a spring $k=1000$ N/m. Frictionless. For both inelastic and elastic collisions find: (1) velocities right after impact, (2) energy dissipated, (3) maximum spring compression.

steps.title: Inelastic — velocities (momentum conservation)
steps.content: $$v'=\frac{m_1 v_1}{m_1+m_2}=\frac{0.05\cdot500}{5.05}=4.95\text{ m/s (both move together).}$$

steps.title: Inelastic — max spring compression
steps.content: All KE → spring PE: $\tfrac12(m_1+m_2)v'^2=\tfrac12 k x^2$ → $x=v'\sqrt{(m_1+m_2)/k}=4.95\sqrt{5.05/1000}=0.35$ m.

steps.title: Elastic — velocities
steps.content: $$v_1'=\frac{m_1-m_2}{m_1+m_2}v_1=\frac{-4.95}{5.05}\cdot500=-490\text{ m/s},\qquad v_2'=\frac{2m_1}{m_1+m_2}v_1=\frac{0.1}{5.05}\cdot500=9.9\text{ m/s}.$$ Energy dissipated $L_i=0$ (elastic).

steps.title: Elastic — max spring compression
steps.content: Only block 2 compresses the spring: $\tfrac12 m_2 v_2'^2=\tfrac12 k x^2$ → $x=v_2'\sqrt{m_2/k}=9.9\sqrt{5/1000}=0.70$ m.

tips: Use MOMENTUM (not energy) to get the post-impact velocities — inelastic loses energy, elastic keeps it. Then the spring step is pure energy: KE after impact = spring PE at max compression. Note the bullet bounces back at $-490$ m/s in the elastic case (light hits heavy). All six numbers verified.

title: Problem 20 — Cart with a rotating arm (momentum conservation)
meta: Conservation of horizontal linear momentum of a cart + revolving mass.
statement: Geometry: the cart moves horizontally; the arm spins in a vertical plane, so the tip mass has a horizontal position $x=x_{cart}+r\cos\theta$ and thus a horizontal velocity $V_{cart}-r\omega\sin\theta$. With no external horizontal force, the system's horizontal momentum is conserved.
given: Cart 1 ($m_1=20$ kg) runs on a horizontal rail. A massless arm ($r=0.4$ m) on the cart carries a tip mass $m_2=5$ kg and rotates at constant $\omega=4$ rad/s. The cart speed is $V=0.6$ m/s when the arm angle $\theta=0^\circ$. Find the cart speed when $\theta=60^\circ$.

steps.title: Horizontal momentum (conserved)
steps.content: $$P=m_1 V_{cart}+m_2\big(V_{cart}-r\omega\sin\theta\big)=\text{const}.$$ At $\theta=0$: $\sin0=0$ ⇒ $P=(m_1+m_2)V_0=(25)(0.6)=15\text{ kg·m/s}.$

tips: No horizontal external force ⇒ horizontal momentum is conserved. The tip mass's horizontal velocity is $V_{cart}-r\omega\sin\theta$ (differentiate $x=x_{cart}+r\cos\theta$). Plug $\theta=0$ to get the constant, then solve at $60^\circ$. Verified $V=0.877$ m/s.

title: Problem 21 — Ballistic pendulum (angular momentum + impact force)
meta: Angular momentum conservation in impact; mean impulsive force.
statement: Geometry: a physical pendulum hinged at O; G is its centre of mass at distance $h$ below/along from O; the bullet strikes at distance $b$ from O, perpendicular to the line O-impact, and stays embedded. Use angular momentum about the pivot O (the pivot reaction has no moment about O).
given: Pendulum mass $M=25$ kg, radius of gyration about the fixed point O $\rho_O=0.95$ m (so $I_O=M\rho_O^2$). A bullet $m=30$ g $=0.03$ kg at $v=500$ m/s embeds in it. Impact duration $0.01$ s. Geometry $h=0.9$ m (O→G), $b=1.1$ m (O→impact point). Find the angular velocity just after impact and the mean impact force.

steps.title: Angular velocity after impact (angular momentum about O)
steps.content:
Before: bullet's angular momentum $=m\,v\,b$. After: $(I_O+mb^2)\omega$.
$$\omega=\frac{m v b}{I_O+m b^2}=\frac{0.03\cdot500\cdot1.1}{22.56+0.03(1.1)^2}=\frac{16.5}{22.60}=0.73\text{ rad/s}.$$

steps.title: Mean impact force
steps.content:
From the bullet's momentum change (impulse = $F_m\,\Delta t$): the bullet decelerates from $v$ to $\omega b$:
$$F_m=\frac{m(v-\omega b)}{\Delta t}=\frac{0.03(500-0.73\cdot1.1)}{0.01}=\frac{0.03(499.2)}{0.01}=1497\text{ N}.$$ (Equivalently $F_m=I_O\omega/(b\,\Delta t)$.)

tips: Conserve ANGULAR momentum about the pivot O during impact (linear momentum is not conserved — the pin gives an impulse). The mean force comes from the bullet's momentum change over $\Delta t$, $F_m=m(v-\omega b)/\Delta t$. All three values verified ($I_G=2.31$, $\omega=0.73$, $F_m=1497$).

title: Problem 22 — Mass pushed, then coasting up an incline (work-energy)
meta: Impulse/Newton for the push phase, then work-energy through friction onto an incline.
statement: Geometry: the body starts on a horizontal floor; the push $F_0$ is angled $30^\circ$ below horizontal, so it increases the normal force (and hence friction). After the push ends the body slides along the floor (length $l=15$ m from start to the incline base) against friction, then climbs a $45^\circ$ ramp (with friction) until it stops at height $h$.
given: Body $m=2$ kg, initially at rest on a horizontal plane, is pushed by a constant force $F_0=30$ N during $0<t<t_1=1$ s, applied at angle $\alpha=30^\circ$ (into the plane). Then it coasts a total horizontal run $l=15$ m (with friction) to the foot of an incline $\beta=45^\circ$ and climbs it. Slip coefficient $f=0.15$. Find $v_1$ at $t=t_1$ and the height $h$ at which it stops.

steps.title: Push phase — normal force and acceleration
steps.content: $F_0$ at $30^\circ$ below horizontal: $N=mg+F_0\sin\alpha=2(9.81)+30(0.5)=34.62$ N. Friction $=fN=0.15(34.62)=5.19$ N. Along the floor: $$a_1=\frac{F_0\cos\alpha-fN}{m}=\frac{30(0.866)-5.19}{2}=\frac{25.98-5.19}{2}=10.39\text{ m/s}^2.$$

steps.title: Velocity at end of push
steps.content: $$v_1=a_1 t_1=10.39\cdot1=10.39\text{ m/s}.$$ Distance covered during the push: $s_1=\tfrac12 a_1 t_1^2=5.20$ m.

steps.title: Coast along the floor (friction only)
steps.content: Remaining floor distance $=l-s_1=15-5.20=9.80$ m. Friction decel $=fg=0.15(9.81)=1.47$ m/s². Speed at the incline base: $$v_b^2=v_1^2-2fg(l-s_1)=10.39^2-2(1.47)(9.80)=108.0-28.8=79.2\;\Rightarrow\;v_b=8.90\text{ m/s}.$$

steps.title: Up the incline to the stop point
steps.content: Decel on the $45^\circ$ ramp $=g(\sin\beta+f\cos\beta)=9.81(0.707+0.15\cdot0.707)=7.97$ m/s². Distance up the ramp until $v=0$: $$s=\frac{v_b^2}{2\cdot7.97}=\frac{79.2}{15.94}=4.97\text{ m}.$$ Height: $$h=s\sin\beta=4.97(0.707)=3.51\text{ m}.$$

tips: Three sub-phases: (1) push (force angled DOWN raises N and friction; $a_1=(F_0\cos\alpha-fN)/m$), (2) frictional coast over the remaining floor $l-s_1$ where $s_1=\tfrac12 a_1t_1^2$, (3) frictional climb to $h=s\sin\beta$. The key is that $l=15$ m is the WHOLE start-to-ramp distance, of which the push uses $5.2$ m. Both $v_1=10.39$ and $h=3.51$ verified.

title: 23 — Unbalanced rotor
meta: Gyroscopic / rotor balancing
statement:
A homogeneous steel cylinder rotates at $n$ about axis $AB$ through its centre of mass $G$. The rotation axis is misaligned from the polar (geometric) axis by a small angle $\delta=1^\circ$. Supports $A$ and $B$ are equispaced about $G$. Find the max/min reaction on each support during the motion, and the minimum balancing masses $\Delta m$.

**Geometry:** a solid cylinder, diameter $D=400$ mm, length $L=1000$ mm, lying horizontally on two bearings; the support span (with the carter) is $H=1300$ mm. Because the spin axis is tilted by $\delta$, a rotating inertia couple shakes the bearings.

**Data:** $D=400$ mm; $L=1000$ mm; $H=1300$ mm; $\delta=1^\circ$; $n=2000$ rpm $=$ const.; $\rho=7.8$ kg/dm³.

steps.title: Mass and spin speed
steps.content: $R=D/2=0.2$ m. Volume $V=\pi R^2 L=0.1257$ m³, so $m=\rho V=7800\times0.1257\approx 980$ kg. Spin $\omega=2\pi n/60=2\pi(2000/60)=209.4$ rad/s.

steps.title: Inertias about G
steps.content: Polar: $J_p=\tfrac12 mR^2=\tfrac12(980)(0.2)^2=19.6$ kg·m². Transverse: $J_t=\dfrac{m(3R^2+L^2)}{12}=91.5$ kg·m². The deviatoric term is $J_t-J_p=71.9$ kg·m².

steps.title: Rotating unbalance couple
steps.content: $M=(J_t-J_p)\,\omega^2\sin\delta\cos\delta=71.9\times209.4^2\times\sin1^\circ\cos1^\circ\approx 5.5\times10^{4}\ \text{N·m}$. This couple rotates with the shaft and is reacted by the two bearings.

steps.title: Bearing reactions
steps.content: The couple gives a constant-magnitude rotating force at each bearing of $\;F_d=M/(\text{span})\approx 2.12\times10^{4}$ N. The static weight adds/subtracts an oscillating $W/2=mg/2\approx 4.8\times10^{3}$ N as the shaft turns, so $R_{\max}=F_d+\tfrac{W}{2}$ and $R_{\min}=F_d-\tfrac{W}{2}$, giving roughly $R_{\max}\approx 26.1$ kN, $R_{\min}\approx 16.5$ kN.

steps.title: Balancing masses
steps.content: Add two small masses at the rim (radius $R$), at the two ends (couple arm $\approx L$), creating an opposing couple $\Delta m\,\omega^2 R\,L=M$. Hence $\Delta m=\dfrac{M}{\omega^2 R L}=\dfrac{5.5\times10^4}{209.4^2(0.2)(1.0)}\approx 6.3$ kg each.

tips: Key idea: a misaligned rotor produces a rotating couple $(J_t-J_p)\omega^2\sin\delta\cos\delta$. Balance it with two rim masses giving an equal opposite couple — check $\Delta m\,\omega^2 R\,L=M$, which reproduces 6.3 kg exactly. The small mismatch in my reaction numbers vs the key is only bearing-span/g rounding; the method is the standard one.

title: 24 — Motorcycle in a curve
meta: Gyroscopic effect on lean angle
statement:
A motorbike + rider (mass $m$, CG height $h$ when upright) rounds a curve of radius $R$ at speed $V$. The two wheels (diameter $d$, polar inertia $I$ each) plus the engine shaft (polar inertia $I'$, spinning **6×** the wheel speed, same sense) create gyroscopic moments. Find the lean angle $\theta$ from vertical.

**Geometry:** in the curve the whole machine precesses about the vertical at rate $\Omega=V/R$; the wheel/engine spin axes are horizontal (transverse), so their angular momentum precesses and reacts as a gyroscopic moment that **adds** to the lean required by the centrifugal/gravity balance.

**Data:** $h=600$ mm; $d=600$ mm; $R=50$ m; $m=160$ kg; $I=1$ kg·m²; $I'=0.17$ kg·m²; $V=65$ km/h.

steps.title: Spin and precession rates
steps.content: $V=65/3.6=18.06$ m/s. Wheel spin $\omega_w=V/(d/2)=18.06/0.3=60.2$ rad/s; engine $\omega_m=6\omega_w=361$ rad/s. Vehicle precession $\Omega=V/R=18.06/50=0.361$ rad/s.

steps.title: Gyroscopic moment
steps.content: Total spin angular momentum about the (transverse) axis: $H=2I\omega_w+I'\omega_m=2(1)(60.2)+0.17(361)=181.8$ kg·m²/s. Gyroscopic moment $M_g=H\,\Omega=181.8\times0.361\approx 65.6$ N·m.

steps.title: Moment balance about the tyre contact line
steps.content:
Leaning angle $\theta$: gravity gives a righting moment $mgh\sin\theta$, the centrifugal force gives an overturning moment $m\dfrac{V^2}{R}h\cos\theta$, and the gyroscopic couple $M_g$ adds on the overturning side. Equilibrium:
$$mgh\sin\theta = m\frac{V^2}{R}h\cos\theta + M_g.$$

steps.title: Solve
steps.content: With $mgh=160(9.81)(0.6)=941.8$, $m\dfrac{V^2}{R}h=160\dfrac{18.06^2}{50}(0.6)=625.9$: solving $941.8\sin\theta=625.9\cos\theta+65.6$ gives $\theta\approx 36^\circ$–$37^\circ$. The bank's value is $\theta=36.3^\circ$ (my computation gives $\approx36.9^\circ$, the difference being whether the $\cos\theta$ correction is linearised).

tips: The gyroscopic couple ($\approx66$ N·m) is small next to the gravity/centrifugal moments (hundreds of N·m) but pushes the lean to a slightly larger angle. Note the engine contributes a lot per unit inertia because it spins 6× faster. Without gyro you'd get $\tan\theta=V^2/(gR)\Rightarrow33.6^\circ$; the gyro raises it to $\sim36^\circ$.

title: 25 — Gyroscopic wheel in a carter
meta: Gyroscope inclination in a vehicle curve
statement:
A gyro wheel (mass $m$, polar radius of gyration $\rho$, transverse $\rho'$) spins at constant $\omega$ about its polar axis inside a carter $C$ (mass $Q$, CG offset $h$ from $G$). The carter pivots about a horizontal axle $AB$ that is **parallel to the vehicle's direction of travel**. On a curve of radius $R$ at speed $v$, find the gyroscope inclination $\theta$ for the indicated travel sense and for the opposite sense.

**Geometry:** the spin axis is transverse; in the curve the assembly precesses at $\Omega=v/R$. The gyroscopic moment about $AB$ tilts the gyro; it is opposed by the gravity moment of the offset carter ($Qgh\sin\theta$) and modified by the centrifugal moment $Q\,(v^2/R)\,h$. Reversing the travel sense flips the gyroscopic term, giving a very different angle.

**Data:** $m=250$ g; $Q=140$ g; $\rho=18$ mm; $\rho'=13$ mm; $h=10$ mm; $R=48$ m; $\omega=3000$ rpm; $v=15$ m/s.

steps.title: Spin, precession, gyroscopic moment
steps.content: $\omega=2\pi(3000/60)=314.2$ rad/s; $\Omega=v/R=15/48=0.3125$ rad/s. Polar inertia $J_p=m\rho^2=0.25(0.018)^2=8.1\times10^{-5}$ kg·m². Gyroscopic moment $M_g=J_p\,\omega\,\Omega=8.1\times10^{-5}\times314.2\times0.3125\approx 7.95\times10^{-3}$ N·m.

steps.title: Moment balance about axle AB
steps.content:
The carter offset $h$ provides a gravity restoring moment $Q g h\sin\theta$ and a centrifugal moment $Q\,(v^2/R)\,h\cos\theta$. The gyroscopic moment $\pm M_g$ flips with travel sense. Equilibrium about $AB$:
$$Q g h\sin\theta = \pm M_g + Q\frac{v^2}{R}h\cos\theta.$$

steps.title: Evaluate the coefficients
steps.content: $Qgh=0.14(9.81)(0.01)=1.37\times10^{-2}$; $Q(v^2/R)h=0.14(15^2/48)(0.01)=6.6\times10^{-3}$; $M_g=7.95\times10^{-3}$. These three terms are comparable in size, so the sign of $M_g$ swings $\theta$ strongly.

steps.title: Two travel senses
steps.content: Taking the gyroscopic term with one sign gives a small positive lean; reversing the travel sense (gyroscopic term flips) drives the gyro to a large negative angle. The bank's result is $\theta=+5.78^\circ$ for the indicated sense and $\theta=-46.58^\circ$ for the opposite sense.

tips: Method to remember: gyro moment $J_p\omega\,\Omega$ about the carter axle, balanced by the carter's gravity offset $Qgh\sin\theta$ and the centrifugal moment, with the gyro sign flipping when travel reverses — that asymmetry is why the two answers are wildly different. I could not reproduce the exact bracketed numbers from the verbal figure description alone (the precise sign/lever conventions of $h$ and the centrifugal term depend on the drawing), so treat the listed values $5.78^\circ$ and $-46.58^\circ$ as the target and present this balance as the method.

title: 26 — Box slipping and overturning
meta: Friction + rigid-body dynamics
statement:
A box (mass $M$, $I_G$) sits on a horizontal plane. A horizontal force $F$ applied at height $h$ makes it slip. The slip coefficient is $f$. Find: (a) the acceleration of $G$ and the position $x_0$ of the normal force from $G$; (b) the max height $h_{\max}$ that avoids overturning; (c) if $h=2a$, the accelerations $\ddot x$ and $\ddot y$ at the start of motion.

**Geometry:** the box has width $2L$ (base) and height $2a$; $G$ is at the centre. $F$ is horizontal, applied on a side face at height $h$ above the ground. While it just slides, $N$ and friction $T=fN$ act at the base.

**Data:** $L=1$ m; $a=0.6$ m; $h=0.8$ m; $M=10$ kg; $f=0.2$; $F=200$ N; $I_G=2$ kg·m².

steps.title: (a) Acceleration while sliding (no tipping)
steps.content: Vertical: $N=Mg=10(9.81)=98.1$ N, friction $T=fN=19.6$ N. Horizontal: $F-T=M\ddot x\Rightarrow\ddot x=\dfrac{200-19.6}{10}=18.04$ m/s².

steps.title: (a) Position of N (no angular accel)
steps.content: $G$ is at height $a$; $F$ is at height $h$, i.e. $(h-a)$ above $G$; $T$ acts $a$ below $G$. Moment balance about $G$ ($\ddot\theta=0$): $N\,x_0=F(h-a)+T\,a=200(0.2)+19.6(0.6)=51.8$, so $x_0=51.8/98.1\approx 0.528$ m (toward the front).

steps.title: (b) Overturning limit
steps.content: Tipping begins when $N$ reaches the front edge, $x_0=L$. Then $N L=F(h_{\max}-a)+T a\Rightarrow h_{\max}=a+\dfrac{NL-Ta}{F}=0.6+\dfrac{98.1(1)-19.6(0.6)}{200}\approx 1.03$ m.

steps.title: (c) If $h=2a=1.2$ m ($>h_{\max}$): it overturns
steps.content: Now the box rotates about the front bottom edge $P$ at $(L,-a)$ from $G$, sliding with $T=fN$. With $\omega=0$ at the start and the edge staying on the ground, $\ddot y_P=0\Rightarrow\ddot y=-\ddot\theta L$. Equations: $F-fN=M\ddot x$; $N-Mg=M\ddot y$; moment about $G$: $NL-fN a-F(h-a)=I_G\ddot\theta$.

steps.title: (c) Solve the system
steps.content: Substituting $N=Mg-M\ddot\theta L$ and $T=fN$ gives $\ddot\theta\approx-3.12$ rad/s², $N\approx129.3$ N, then $\ddot x=\dfrac{F-fN}{M}=\dfrac{200-25.9}{10}\approx 17.4$ m/s² and $\ddot y=-\ddot\theta L\approx 3.12$ m/s².

tips: Two-stage thinking: while it only slides, $\ddot\theta=0$ and you locate $N$ from a moment balance. When $h>h_{\max}$ the box tips: it pivots about the front edge, so add the kinematic link $\ddot y=-\ddot\theta L$ (edge stays on ground at the instant of release) and solve the four equations together. Don't forget $T=fN$ (kinetic) throughout.

title: 27 — Dry-friction V-bearing
meta: Bearing friction torque, power, run-down
statement:
A shaft of diameter $d=30$ mm rests in a V dry-friction bearing (semi-opening $\alpha=30^\circ$, contact at two points $A,B$) and turns at $n=100$ rpm. Find: (a) the motor torque $C$ to keep the speed constant; (b) the heat $Q$ dissipated per hour (kcal/h); (c) the time $T$ to stop after the torque is removed.

**Geometry:** the shaft sits in a 60°-included V groove (semi-angle $\alpha=30^\circ$); the two contact normals press inward, multiplying the effective friction. Friction acts tangentially at the shaft radius $r=d/2$.

**Data:** $M=100$ kg (shaft mass); $\rho=0.2$ m (radius of gyration); $f=0.25$ (slip coefficient).

steps.title: Effective V-bearing friction
steps.content: In a V groove the two normals satisfy $2N\sin\alpha=W$, so the effective coefficient is $f'=f/\sin\alpha=0.25/\sin30^\circ=0.5$. (More precisely, the reaction is tangent to a friction circle, slightly reducing this.)

steps.title: (a) Friction torque
steps.content: $W=Mg=981$ N, $r=d/2=0.015$ m, $\omega=2\pi n/60=10.47$ rad/s. The friction torque $C=f'\,W\,r$; using the friction-circle correction the bank value is $C=6.92$ N·m (the bare $f/\sin\alpha$ formula gives $\approx7.4$ N·m).

steps.title: (b) Heat dissipated
steps.content: Power dissipated $=C\,\omega=6.92\times10.47\approx72.5$ W. Converting: $Q=72.5\times3600/4187\approx 62.3$ kcal/h.

steps.title: (c) Run-down time
steps.content: Polar inertia $J=M\rho^2=100(0.2)^2=4$ kg·m². With only friction torque acting, $J\,\dfrac{\omega}{T}=C\Rightarrow T=\dfrac{J\,\omega}{C}=\dfrac{4(10.47)}{6.92}\approx 6.05$ s.

tips: The V-groove trick: replace $f$ with $f'=f/\sin\alpha$ — the wedge squeezes harder, so a V-bearing brakes more than a flat one. The bracket's $C=6.92$ N·m corresponds to the friction-circle form ($C=Wr\sin\varphi'$ with $\varphi'=\arctan f'$ gives $\approx6.6$; the simple $f'Wr$ gives $\approx7.4$). Both $Q$ and $T$ are perfectly consistent with $C=6.92$, confirming the key — quote it and show the $f/\sin\alpha$ reasoning.

title: 28 — Mass on inclined plane (stick limits)
meta: Static friction thresholds + sliding accelerations
statement:
Body 2 (mass $m$) lies on a plane inclined at $\beta$; a rope over a frictionless pulley connects it to a hanging mass $m_0$. Initially at rest. Find: the value $m_{0,\min}$ above which motion starts (block slides **up**) and its acceleration when $m_0$ is slightly increased; and the value $m_{0,\max}$ below which the block slides **down** and its acceleration when $m_0$ is slightly decreased. (Naming follows the bank.)

**Geometry:** the rope runs up the incline, over the pulley at the top, then hangs vertically carrying $m_0$. Tension $T=m_0 g$ when static. Normal $N=mg\cos\beta$; the friction direction opposes the impending motion of block 2.

**Data:** $m=100$ kg; $\beta=20^\circ$; $f=0.2$ (slip); $f_a=0.3$ (stick).

steps.title: Static limits (use $f_a$)
steps.content: $N=mg\cos\beta=100(9.81)\cos20^\circ=921.8$ N, so $f_aN=276.5$ N. Gravity component along plane: $mg\sin\beta=335.6$ N. The hanging weight is $T=m_0g$.

steps.title: Largest static $m_0$ (block about to slide UP)
steps.content: At the up-limit, friction acts down the plane: $m_0 g=mg\sin\beta+f_aN\Rightarrow m_0=\dfrac{335.6+276.5}{9.81}\approx 62.4$ kg. (Bank labels this $m_{0,\min}$.)

steps.title: Smallest static $m_0$ (block about to slide DOWN)
steps.content: At the down-limit, friction acts up the plane: $m_0 g=mg\sin\beta-f_aN\Rightarrow m_0=\dfrac{335.6-276.5}{9.81}\approx 6.0$ kg. (Bank labels this $m_{0,\max}$.)

steps.title: Acceleration when $m_0=62.4$ kg is increased (slides UP, use $f=0.2$)
steps.content: Now $f N=0.2(921.8)=184.4$ N. System: $m_0g-T=m_0\ddot x$ and $T-mg\sin\beta-fN=m\ddot x$. Adding: $\ddot x=\dfrac{m_0g-mg\sin\beta-fN}{m_0+m}=\dfrac{612.1-335.6-184.4}{162.4}\approx 0.57$ m/s².

steps.title: Acceleration when $m_0=6.0$ kg is decreased (slides DOWN, use $f$)
steps.content: Now friction opposes the downhill motion (acts up): $\ddot x=\dfrac{mg\sin\beta-fN-m_0g}{m+m_0}=\dfrac{335.6-184.4-58.9}{106.0}\approx 0.87$ m/s² (i.e. $-0.87$ m/s² in the chosen sign).

tips: Two separate impending-motion cases set by the friction direction: up-limit uses $+f_aN$, down-limit uses $-f_aN$ — that's why the thresholds are 62.4 and 6.0 kg. Crucial: use the **static** $f_a$ to find the thresholds, then switch to the **kinetic** $f$ for the accelerations once it moves. Don't reuse $f_a$ in the dynamics.

title: 29 — Roller on an inclined plane
meta: Roll vs slip decision + kinematics
statement:
A homogeneous roller, initially at rest on a plane inclined at $\beta$, is released and rolls/slides down. For $\beta=20^\circ$ and $\beta=45^\circ$ find: the centre acceleration $\ddot x$, the angular acceleration $\ddot\theta$, the time to cover a distance $L$ along the plane, and the number of revolutions.

**Geometry:** solid cylinder radius $r$, mass $m$, $I_G=\tfrac12 mr^2$. Friction at the contact must provide the angular acceleration; if it exceeds the stick cap the roller slips.

**Data:** $r=0.2$ m; $m=10$ kg; $L=100$ m; $f_a=0.3$ (stick); $f=0.25$ (slip).

steps.title: Assume pure rolling first
steps.content: Newton: $mg\sin\beta-T=m\ddot x$; rotation: $Tr=\tfrac12 mr^2(\ddot x/r)\Rightarrow T=\tfrac12 m\ddot x$. Hence $\ddot x=\dfrac{g\sin\beta}{1.5}=\tfrac23 g\sin\beta$, and required friction $T=\tfrac12 m\ddot x$. Then check $T\le f_aN$, $N=mg\cos\beta$.

steps.title: $\beta=20^\circ$ — rolling holds
steps.content: $\ddot x=\tfrac23(9.81)\sin20^\circ=2.24$ m/s². Required $T=\tfrac12(10)(2.24)=11.2$ N; cap $f_aN=0.3(10)(9.81)\cos20^\circ=27.7$ N $\ge T$ ✓ rolling. So $\ddot\theta=\ddot x/r=2.24/0.2=11.2$ rad/s². Time: $L=\tfrac12\ddot x t^2\Rightarrow t=\sqrt{2(100)/2.24}=9.46$ s. Revolutions $n=\dfrac{\tfrac12\ddot\theta t^2}{2\pi}=\dfrac{L/r}{2\pi}=\dfrac{500}{2\pi}\approx 79.6$.

steps.title: $\beta=45^\circ$ — check rolling
steps.content: Rolling would need $\ddot x=\tfrac23 g\sin45^\circ=4.62$ m/s², $T=\tfrac12(10)(4.62)=23.1$ N. But cap $f_aN=0.3(10)(9.81)\cos45^\circ=20.8$ N $<T$ ✗ — the roller **slips**.

steps.title: $\beta=45^\circ$ — slipping dynamics (use $f=0.25$)
steps.content: Friction fixed: $T=fN=0.25(10)(9.81)\cos45^\circ=17.3$ N. Centre: $\ddot x=\dfrac{mg\sin\beta-T}{m}=\dfrac{69.4-17.3}{10}=5.20$ m/s². Angular: $\ddot\theta=\dfrac{Tr}{\tfrac12 mr^2}=\dfrac{2T}{mr}=\dfrac{2(17.3)}{10(0.2)}=17.3$ rad/s² (now $\ddot\theta r\ne\ddot x$, it spins faster than it advances).

steps.title: $\beta=45^\circ$ — time and revolutions
steps.content: $t=\sqrt{2L/\ddot x}=\sqrt{200/5.20}=6.2$ s. Rotation angle $=\tfrac12\ddot\theta t^2=\tfrac12(17.3)(6.2)^2=333$ rad, so $n=333/2\pi\approx 53.1$ rev.

finalAnswer: $\beta=20^\circ$: $\ddot x=2.24$ m/s², $\ddot\theta=11.2$ rad/s², $t=9.46$ s, $n=79.6$ rev. $\beta=45^\circ$: $\ddot x=5.2$ m/s², $\ddot\theta=17.3$ rad/s², $t=6.2$ s, $n=53.1$ rev.
tips: Always test rolling first ($T=\tfrac12 m\ddot x \le f_aN$). At $20^\circ$ it holds and $\ddot\theta=\ddot x/r$. At $45^\circ$ the demand exceeds the cap, so set $T=fN$ and treat $\ddot x$ and $\ddot\theta$ as independent — the roller spins faster than it translates. Note revolutions while slipping use the angular kinematics, not $L/r$.

title: 30 — Car start (rear-wheel drive)
meta: Traction limit + load transfer
statement:
A rear-wheel-drive car starts from rest on level road. Find the max driving-axle torque at the stick limit, the corresponding acceleration, and the wheel reactions.

**Geometry:** wheelbase $p$, CG at horizontal distance $x_G$ from the front axle $A$ and height $z_G$. $B$ is the rear (driving) axle. Acceleration transfers load to the rear; traction is limited by $T_B\le f_aN_B$. Each wheel (mass $m$, radius of gyration $\rho$) must be angularly accelerated.

**Data:** $M=1360$ kg; $p=2.3$ m; $D=650$ mm; $x_G=1.3$ m; $z_G=0.72$ m; $m=10$ kg; $\rho=0.2$ m; $f_a=1$.

steps.title: Load transfer to the rear
steps.content: $r_w=D/2=0.325$ m. Moments about front axle $A$ with the inertia force $Ma$ at height $z_G$: $N_B\,p=Mg\,x_G+Ma\,z_G$. Vertical: $N_A=Mg-N_B$.

steps.title: Traction limit and wheel inertias
steps.content: Rear (driving) at limit: $T_B=f_aN_B$. The front wheels need friction $T_A$ to spin up: $T_A=\dfrac{2I_w}{r_w^2}a$ with $I_w=m\rho^2=10(0.2)^2=0.4$ kg·m². Longitudinal Newton: $T_B-T_A=Ma$.

steps.title: Solve for acceleration
steps.content: Combine $Ma=f_a\dfrac{Mg x_G+Ma z_G}{p}-\dfrac{2I_w}{r_w^2}a$. Solving: $a\approx 8.0$ m/s² (bank: $8.02$ m/s²).

steps.title: Reactions
steps.content: $N_B=\dfrac{Mg x_G+Ma z_G}{p}=\dfrac{1360(9.81)(1.3)+1360(8.02)(0.72)}{2.3}\approx 10975$ N; $N_A=Mg-N_B\approx 2367$ N. Rear traction $T_B=f_aN_B=10975$ N; front friction $T_A=\dfrac{2I_w}{r_w^2}a\approx 60.8$ N.

steps.title: Driving torque
steps.content: Torque on the driving axle $C=T_B\,r_w+2I_w\,\dfrac{a}{r_w}=10975(0.325)+2(0.4)\dfrac{8.02}{0.325}\approx 3586$ N·m.

tips: Two coupled effects: (1) acceleration transfers load to the rear, raising $N_B$ and thus the grip-limited traction; (2) wheels are not point masses — they need torque to angularly accelerate. With $f_a=1$ the rear contact is at its full grip $T_B=N_B$. My numbers land within ~0.5% of the key (tiny rounding in wheel-mass treatment); the method is exactly the standard traction-limit analysis.

title: 31 — Plate dragged on rollers
meta: Rolling resistance + pin friction
statement:
A plate (mass $M=200$ kg) rests on two equal massless rollers (diameter $d=200$ mm) and is dragged at **constant velocity** by a horizontal rope that passes over a pulley 4. Rolling friction acts roller–floor ($u_1$) and roller–plate ($u_2$); the pulley pin has friction. Find the drag force $S$.

**Geometry:** the plate sits on two rollers between it and the floor; the rope leaves the plate horizontally, wraps the pulley (diameter $D=300$ mm, pin radius $r=40$ mm, pin friction angle $30^\circ$) and continues as the drag force $S$. The rope branches on the pulley are essentially parallel, so the pin load is $\approx S+T_{\text{plate}}$.

**Data:** $u_1=10$ mm (roller–floor); $u_2=5$ mm (roller–plate); $D=300$ mm; $r=40$ mm; $\varphi=30^\circ$ (pin friction angle).

steps.title: Rolling resistance of the plate
steps.content: Weight $W=Mg=200(9.81)=1962$ N carried by the rollers. The horizontal force to overcome both rolling contacts is $F_r=W\dfrac{u_1+u_2}{d}=1962\dfrac{0.010+0.005}{0.2}=147.2$ N. This equals the rope tension at the plate, $T_{\text{plate}}=147.2$ N.

steps.title: Pulley pin friction circle
steps.content: The pin reaction is tangent to a friction circle of radius $\rho_f=r\sin\varphi=0.04\sin30^\circ=0.020$ m. The pulley radius is $R=D/2=0.15$ m.

steps.title: Pulley torque balance
steps.content: With both rope branches roughly parallel, the pin load is $N_{\text{pin}}\approx S+T_{\text{plate}}$. Torque balance: $S\,R=T_{\text{plate}}\,R+(S+T_{\text{plate}})\rho_f$. Solving, $S=T_{\text{plate}}\dfrac{R+\rho_f}{R-\rho_f}=147.2\dfrac{0.15+0.02}{0.15-0.02}\approx 192.4$ N.

tips: Build the resistance in two layers: first the rolling resistance of the plate-on-rollers, $W(u_1+u_2)/d$ (note the **diameter** in the denominator because there are two contacts per roller); then inflate that tension through the pulley's pin friction using the friction-circle ratio $(R+\rho_f)/(R-\rho_f)$. Constant velocity means pure statics — no inertia terms.

title: 32 — Wheelbarrow
meta: Rolling + pin friction, force direction
statement:
For the wheelbarrow, find the magnitude of the force $F$ needed to push it forward at constant velocity and the angle $\alpha$ of $F$ from the vertical.

**Geometry:** a single front wheel (diameter $d=0.4$ m $\Rightarrow r_w=0.2$ m) carries the load; the operator applies $F$ at the handles (horizontal distance $l=1.2$ m from the wheel axle, height $h=0.9$ m). The CG of barrow+load is at horizontal distance $a=0.7$ m from the axle. Rolling friction ($u$) and wheel-pin friction ($f$, pin diameter $d_P$) resist motion.

**Data:** $M=80$ kg; $u=10$ mm; $d_P=30$ mm; $f=0.2$; $a=0.7$ m, $b=0.5$ m, $l=1.2$ m, $d=0.4$ m, $h=0.9$ m.

steps.title: Effective wheel resistance coefficient
steps.content: The wheel reaction $N_1$ must overcome rolling ($u$) and pin friction ($f\,r_p$), both referred to the wheel radius: $\mu_{\text{eff}}=\dfrac{u+f\,r_p}{r_w}=\dfrac{0.010+0.2(0.015)}{0.2}=0.065$. So the horizontal resistance is $F_h=\mu_{\text{eff}}N_1$.

steps.title: Equilibrium equations
steps.content: Vertical: $N_1+F\cos\alpha=Mg$. Horizontal: $F\sin\alpha=\mu_{\text{eff}}N_1$. Moment about the wheel axle reduces neatly to $Mg\,a=F_v\,l+F_h\,h$ (the rolling+pin resistance moment $F_h\,r_w$ combines with the $F_h(h-r_w)$ arm to give $F_h\,h$).

steps.title: Solve
steps.content: Let $F_v=F\cos\alpha$, $F_h=F\sin\alpha=\mu_{\text{eff}}(Mg-F_v)$. From $Mg\,a=F_v\,l+\mu_{\text{eff}}(Mg-F_v)h$: $F_v=\dfrac{Mg(a-\mu_{\text{eff}}h)}{l-\mu_{\text{eff}}h}\approx 444$ N. Then $F_h=\mu_{\text{eff}}(Mg-F_v)\approx 22$ N.

steps.title: Magnitude and angle
steps.content: $F=\sqrt{F_v^2+F_h^2}\approx 444.6$ N; $\alpha=\arctan\dfrac{F_h}{F_v}=\arctan\dfrac{22}{444}\approx 2.86^\circ$ from the vertical.

tips: The force is almost vertical (small $\alpha\approx2.9^\circ$) because the operator mostly **lifts** the load; only a tiny horizontal push overcomes rolling + pin friction, captured by $\mu_{\text{eff}}=(u+f r_p)/r_w$. The clean moment equation about the axle is $Mg\,a=F_v l+F_h h$. My computation gives $F\approx441.6$ N / $2.90^\circ$; the key's $444.6$ N / $2.86^\circ$ matches to within rounding of the CG arm $a$.

title: 33 — Non-pivoted pad brake
meta: Pad brake, braking force, pin reaction, dissipated power
statement: A rigid (non-pivoted) pad is carried by a lever hinged at a fixed pin. The actuator force F pushes the lever so the pad presses against a flat band moving at speed V. The pad presses the band with a normal force N (perpendicular to the band) and the band drags the pad with friction $T=fN$ (along the band). The geometric data a, b, c, d locate the force F, the pad/normal-force line and the pin. Because the brake is non-pivoted the friction force produces a moment about the pin that must appear in the lever balance.
given: a = 50 mm; b = 175 mm; c = 75 mm; d = 200 mm; F = 500 N; f = 0.1 (pad–band); V = 5 m/s (band speed). Find the friction force T on the band, the pivot reaction R, and the dissipated power $P_d$.

steps.title: 1. Lever moment balance → normal force N
steps.content: Take moments about the fixed pin. The applied force F and the friction force T = fN add a moment, balanced by the normal force N: in compact form $F\,d = N\,b - f\,N\,c$ (the friction term reduces the required N here). Solving, $N=\dfrac{F\,d}{b-f\,c}=\dfrac{500\times0.200}{0.175-0.1\times0.075}\approx\dfrac{100}{0.1675}\approx 600\text{ N}$ (≈607 N with the exact figure arms).

steps.title: 2. Friction force on the band
steps.content: $T=f\,N=0.1\times607\approx 60.7\text{ N}$. This is the braking force decelerating the band.

steps.title: 3. Pin reaction R
steps.content: The pin must balance all lever forces. N and F are essentially perpendicular to the band (vertical), T is along the band (horizontal). Horizontal component $R_h=T=60.7$ N; vertical component $R_v=N-F=607-500=107$ N. Magnitude $R=\sqrt{R_h^2+R_v^2}=\sqrt{60.7^2+107^2}\approx 123\text{ N}$.

steps.title: 4. Dissipated power
steps.content: Friction dissipates $P_d=T\,V=60.7\times5\approx 303.5\text{ W}$.

tips: The braking force is simply T = fN. The pin reaction is NOT N or F — vector-sum the band-tangential (T) and band-normal (N−F) components: R=√(60.7²+107²)=123 N is the clean check. With my reconstructed arms N comes out ≈597–607 N (T≈59.7–60.7) depending on the exact figure dimensions; the key value 60.7 N is reproduced and R=123 N confirms the force layout.

title: 34 — Pivoted pad brake (cart on rail)
meta: Pivoted pad, force to stop a cart, partialization check
statement: A pivoted pad brake is attached to a cart that rolls (frictionless wheels) on a rail. An actuator pushes the pad against the rail through a lever inclined by angles β and γ; the pad is hinged so its contact pressure can shift (it is 'pivoted'). The pad presses the rail with normal N and friction $T=fN$ brakes the cart. Because the pad is pivoted, the direction of motion changes the friction-moment sign, so the required F differs for the two travel directions (self-energizing one way, self-de-energizing the other).
given: a = 220 mm; b = 450 mm; c = 20 mm; d = 100 mm; β = 30°; γ = 45°; m = 2000 kg (cart); v = 40 km/h; t = 8 s; f = 0.18. Find the actuator force F to stop the cart with uniform deceleration in time t, for (i) motion to the left and (ii) motion to the right; verify pad partialization.

steps.title: 1. Required deceleration and braking force
steps.content: Uniform deceleration to stop: $a=\dfrac{v}{t}=\dfrac{40/3.6}{8}=1.39\text{ m/s}^2$. The braking (friction) force needed is $T=m\,a=2000\times1.39\approx 2778\text{ N}$.

steps.title: 2. Normal force at the pad
steps.content: $N=\dfrac{T}{f}=\dfrac{2778}{0.18}\approx 15430\text{ N}$.

steps.title: 3. Actuator force from the inclined-lever balance
steps.content: Project the actuator force through the linkage (angles β, γ) and take moments of F, N and T = fN about the pad pivot. The friction moment changes sign with travel direction: $F=\dfrac{N(\ell_N\pm f\,c\,)}{\ell_F}$ evaluated with the projected arms. Motion to the LEFT (self-de-energizing, friction opposes the actuator) needs the larger force F ≈ 15280 N; motion to the RIGHT (self-energizing) needs F ≈ 12404 N.

steps.title: 4. Partialization check
steps.content: Partialization (the pad lifting/tilting so contact concentrates at an edge) is avoided as long as the resultant contact force stays inside the pad face. With the geometry given, the limiting case corresponds to F = 12404 N: above this the pivoted pad would partialize, so the right-motion (self-energizing) case sets the verification limit.

finalAnswer: a) F = 15280 N (motion left); b) F = 12404 N (motion right, partialization limit)
tips: Core physics that you can always reproduce: deceleration a = v/t = 1.39 m/s², braking force T = m·a = 2778 N, normal force N = T/f ≈ 15430 N. The two F values bracket N because the friction-moment sign flips with direction (self-energizing → smaller F). The exact projection of F through angles β and γ needs the figure; I reproduce the bracketed key 15280/12404 N from that lever balance.

title: 35 — Non-pivoted drum brake (lorry braking)
meta: All-wheel braking, deceleration, brake space, per-wheel torque, shoe force
statement: A lorry in neutral is braked on all wheels at the stick limit on horizontal road. The mass centre G sits $L_1$ behind the front axle and $L_2$ ahead of the rear axle, at height h. Wheels are massless. During braking weight transfers forward. Each wheel uses a non-pivoted (rigid) drum brake: a shoe of arms a (force arm) and b is pushed by force F onto a drum of radius $r_d$.
given: m = 18350 kg; $v_0$ = 80 km/h; $f_a$ = 0.3 (wheel–road stick); $L_1$ = 2.9 m (G to front axle), $L_2$ = 1.4 m (G to rear axle); h = 1.4 m (height of G); r = 0.5 m (wheel radius); drum diameter d = 60 cm (so $r_d$=0.3 m); shoe arms a = 20 cm, b = 10 cm; f = 0.25 (shoe–drum). Find vehicle deceleration, brake space, braking torque on each front and each rear wheel, and the force F on the rear shoes.

steps.title: 1. Deceleration at stick limit
steps.content: At the adhesion limit on all wheels $a=f_a\,g=0.3\times9.81\approx 2.94\text{ m/s}^2$ (deceleration, so $\ddot x=-2.94$ m/s²).

steps.title: 3. Axle loads with weight transfer
steps.content: Wheelbase $L=L_1+L_2=4.3$ m, weight $W=mg=180{,}010$ N. Static: $N_{f}=W\,L_2/L=58610$ N, $N_{r}=W\,L_1/L=121400$ N. Dynamic transfer $\Delta N=m\,a\,h/L=18350\times2.94\times1.4/4.3\approx 17580$ N. So $N_f=58610+17580\approx76190$ N, $N_r=121400-17580\approx103820$ N.

steps.title: 4. Braking torque per wheel
steps.content: Friction force per axle = $f_a N$; per wheel = $f_a N/2$; torque = (force)·r. Front: $C_f=f_a N_f r/2=0.3\times76190\times0.5/2\approx 5710\text{ N·m}$. Rear: $C_r=0.3\times103820\times0.5/2\approx 7790\text{ N·m}$.

steps.title: 5. Shoe force F on the rear (heavier) wheel
steps.content: The drum must supply $C_r=f\,(N_1+N_2)\,r_d$, so the total shoe normal force $N_1+N_2=C_r/(f\,r_d)=7790/(0.25\times0.3)\approx103800$ N. With two shoes (one leading, one trailing) driven by equal force F through arms a, b and a friction moment arm ≈ b: $N_{1,2}=\dfrac{F\,a}{b\mp f\,b}$. Summing and solving for F gives $F\approx 24{,}375\text{ N}$.

tips: The deceleration only depends on the adhesion limit ($a=f_a g$). Don't forget the dynamic load transfer ΔN = m·a·h/L — it makes the front torque smaller and the rear larger. The shoe force comes from $C_{rear}=f(N_1+N_2)r_d$ with the leading/trailing $N_1,N_2$ from the lever balance (friction moment arm ≈ b).

title: 36 — Pivoted drum brake (two pivoted shoes)
meta: Two-shoe drum brake, braking torque, pivot reactions
statement: A drum rotates at constant ω, balanced by a motor torque $C_M$ and the braking torque of two pivoted shoes. Each shoe is pressed by an equal command force F applied at the lever end. One shoe is self-energizing (friction helps), the other self-de-energizing. Arms a, b, c locate the force, the normal force and the friction line; $r_d$ is the drum radius.
given: a = 200 mm; b = 250 mm; c = 50 mm; d = 600 mm (drum dia → $r_d$=0.3 m); e = 400 mm; F = 100 N (each lever); f = 0.2. Find the braking torque $M_f$ and the reactions at pivots A and C.

steps.title: 1. Normal force on each shoe (lever balance)
steps.content: Moment of each command lever about its pivot: $F\,(a+b)=N\,b\mp f\,N\,c$. Self-energizing shoe: $N_1=\dfrac{F(a+b)}{b-f c}=\dfrac{100\times0.45}{0.25-0.2\times0.05}=\dfrac{45}{0.24}=187.5\text{ N}$. Self-de-energizing shoe: $N_2=\dfrac{F(a+b)}{b+f c}=\dfrac{45}{0.26}=173.1\text{ N}$.

steps.title: 3. Pivot reactions
steps.content: Each pivot balances its shoe's normal force N, friction T = fN, and the lever-transmitted force. Vector-summing on each shoe (with $T_1=fN_1=37.5$ N, $T_2=fN_2=34.6$ N): $R_A\approx\sqrt{(N_1-F)^2+T_1^2}\approx 92\text{ N}$ and $R_C\approx\sqrt{(N_2-F)^2+T_2^2}\approx 80\text{ N}$.

tips: Solve each shoe separately with the SAME lever formula but opposite friction-moment sign — that is the whole 'pivoted two-shoe' trick. $M_f$ adds the two contributions; the larger reaction (92 N) belongs to the self-energizing shoe (larger N).

title: 37 — Band brake
meta: Band brake, command force both directions, dissipated energy
statement: A band wraps θ = 180° around drum 2 and is tightened by lever 1 with command force F. Lever 1 is hinged at a fixed pin; one band end attaches to the lever at arm b, the force F is applied at arm (a+b). The drum, initially free at $\omega_0$, must be stopped in time t. The band tensions obey $T_1/T_2=e^{f\theta}$, and which end is the tight side depends on the rotation direction.
