given: a = 100 mm; b = 400 mm; r = 200 mm; θ = 180° = π rad; f = 0.3; drum M = 50 kg, ρ = 100 mm (inertial radius); $\omega_0$ = 100 rad/s, stopped in t = 2 s. Find the command force F and the dissipated energy for (a) clockwise and (b) anticlockwise drum rotation.

steps.title: 1. Braking torque needed
steps.content: Drum inertia $I=M\rho^2=50\times0.1^2=0.5\text{ kg·m}^2$. Angular deceleration $\dot\omega=\omega_0/t=100/2=50\text{ rad/s}^2$. Required torque $M_f=I\dot\omega=0.5\times50=25\text{ N·m}$.

steps.title: 3. Command force (lever balance, both directions)
steps.content: Lever moment: $F\,(a+b)=T_{end}\,b$, i.e. $F=T_{end}\,\dfrac{b}{a+b}=T_{end}\times\dfrac{0.4}{0.5}=0.8\,T_{end}$. The band end attached to the lever is the slack side $T_2$ for anticlockwise rotation and the tight side $T_1$ for clockwise rotation (self-energizing the other way). Anticlockwise: $F_{ant}=0.8\times79.8\approx 63.8\text{ N}$. Clockwise: $F_{or}=0.8\times204.8\approx 163.8\text{ N}$.

steps.title: 4. Dissipated energy
steps.content: All kinetic energy becomes heat: $L_i=\tfrac12 I\omega_0^2=\tfrac12\times0.5\times100^2= 2500\text{ J}$ (dissipated, $L_i=-2500$ J).

tips: Note θ in radians (180°=π). The dramatic difference between the two directions (63.8 vs 163.8 N) is the self-energizing effect: when the lever pulls the slack end the brake helps itself. The lever ratio here is b/(a+b)=0.8. Dissipated energy is just the initial KE and is the same for both directions.

title: 38 — Non-pivoted disc brake
meta: Disc brake, axial force to stop a rotor
statement: A flat annular pad presses axially with force F on a rotating disc (rotor). The pad covers from inner radius $R_i$ to outer radius $R_e$. Friction torque $M_f=f\,F\,R_m$ acts at the mean radius and decelerates the rotor uniformly to rest in 10 s.
given: M = 100 kg (rotor); ρ = 0.3 m (inertial radius); $\omega_0$ = 1500 rpm; $R_i$ = 15 cm, $R_e$ = 20 cm (pad radii); f = 0.3. Find the force F to stop the rotor in 10 s.

steps.title: 1. Rotor inertia and target torque
steps.content: $I=M\rho^2=100\times0.3^2=9\text{ kg·m}^2$. $\omega_0=1500\times2\pi/60=157.1\text{ rad/s}$. Angular deceleration $\dot\omega=\omega_0/t=157.1/10=15.71\text{ rad/s}^2$. Required torque $M_f=I\dot\omega=9\times15.71\approx 141.4\text{ N·m}$.

steps.title: 3. Axial force
steps.content: With one friction surface, $M_f=f\,F\,R_m$ ⇒ $F=\dfrac{M_f}{f R_m}=\dfrac{141.4}{0.3\times0.175}\approx 2693\text{ N}\approx 2.69\text{ kN}$.

tips: Disc brakes have no self-energizing, so the chain is clean: $M_f=I\dot\omega$ then $F=M_f/(fR_m)$ with $R_m=(R_i+R_e)/2$. This problem uses a single friction interface; if a caliper pressed both faces you would divide by 2.

title: 39 — Multiple-disc clutch
meta: Multi-plate clutch, axial force, pressures, accel/decel limits
statement: A multi-plate clutch with N=6 friction interfaces transmits engine power to a user. Uniform-wear pressure distribution applies. Force F clamps the stack; the transmittable torque is $C=N f F R_m$. In Part 2 the engaged clutch can transmit up to the stick-limit torque before slipping.
given: N = 6 active surfaces; $d_e$ = 140 mm, $d_i$ = 90 mm ($R_e$=70, $R_i$=45 mm); f = 0.3 (slip), $f_a$ = 0.4 (stick). Part 1: minimum axial force F to transmit P = 8 kW at n = 750 rpm, and $p_{max},p_{min}$ during slipping. Part 2: with that F, clutch engaged (sticking), user torque $C_U$ = 40 N·m const, driven-shaft inertia I = 0.06 kg·m², find max acceleration and max deceleration at the stick limit.

steps.title: 1. Torque to transmit and mean radius
steps.content: $\omega=750\times2\pi/60=78.54\text{ rad/s}$; $C=P/\omega=8000/78.54\approx101.9\text{ N·m}$. Uniform wear $R_m=(R_e+R_i)/2=(0.07+0.045)/2=0.0575\text{ m}$.

steps.title: 2. Minimum axial force
steps.content: Using the slip coefficient as the limit, $C=N f F R_m$ ⇒ $F=\dfrac{C}{N f R_m}=\dfrac{101.9}{6\times0.3\times0.0575}\approx 986\text{ N}$.

steps.title: 4. Part 2 — stick-limit torque
steps.content: Engaged (sticking), the clutch can transmit up to $C^*=N f_a F R_m=6\times0.4\times986\times0.0575\approx 136\text{ N·m}$ before it slips.

steps.title: 5. Max acceleration and deceleration
steps.content: Driven shaft: $I\dot\omega=C_{clutch}-C_U$. Max acceleration (clutch drives forward at limit): $\dot\omega_{max}=\dfrac{C^*-C_U}{I}=\dfrac{136-40}{0.06}\approx 1600\text{ rad/s}^2$. Max deceleration (clutch torque reverses at limit): $\dot\omega_{min}=\dfrac{-C^*-C_U}{I}=\dfrac{-136-40}{0.06}\approx -2930\text{ rad/s}^2$.

finalAnswer: F = 986 N; $p_{max}$ = 0.14 MPa; $p_{min}$ = 0.089 MPa; $\dot\omega_{max}$ = 1600 rad/s²; $\dot\omega_{min}$ = −2930 rad/s²
tips: Two coefficients are in play: the minimum clamping force uses the SLIP f (the conservative transmit limit), while the engaged stick-limit torque $C^*$ uses $f_a$. Uniform wear gives $p_{max}$ at the inner radius. For Part 2 the sign of the clutch torque flips between drive (accel) and overrun (decel).

title: 40 — Conical clutch
meta: Cone clutch, axial force, internal/external radii from max pressure
statement: An engine and a user are joined by a conical clutch (half-angle α). The axial force F is amplified by the wedge to a normal force $Q=F/\sin\alpha$, giving friction torque $C=fFR_m/\sin\alpha$. The clutch must transmit the full motor torque; the contact band (from $r_i$ to $r_e$ about the mean radius) is sized so the pressure does not exceed $p_m$.
given: n = 3400 rpm, P = 9 kW (max engine torque point); cone half-angle α = 15°; mean diameter $d_m$ = 100 mm ($R_m$=50 mm); max admissible pressure $p_m$ = 350 kPa; f = 0.2 (slip). Find the minimum axial force F to transmit all the motor torque during slipping, and the inner/outer clutch radii.

steps.title: 1. Motor torque
steps.content: $\omega=3400\times2\pi/60=356.0\text{ rad/s}$; $C=P/\omega=9000/356.0\approx 25.3\text{ N·m}$.

steps.title: 2. Axial force
steps.content: Conical clutch: $C=\dfrac{f F R_m}{\sin\alpha}$ ⇒ $F=\dfrac{C\,\sin\alpha}{f R_m}=\dfrac{25.3\times\sin15^\circ}{0.2\times0.05}=\dfrac{25.3\times0.2588}{0.01}\approx 655\text{ N}$.

steps.title: 3. Inner and outer radii from max pressure
steps.content: With the pressure limit, the axial force is $F=\pi p_m (R_e^2-R_i^2)$. Hence $R_e^2-R_i^2=\dfrac{F}{\pi p_m}=\dfrac{655}{\pi\times350000}=5.95\times10^{-4}\text{ m}^2$. Combined with $R_e+R_i=2R_m=0.100$ m: $R_e-R_i=\dfrac{5.95\times10^{-4}}{0.100}=5.95\times10^{-3}$ m. Solving the pair: $R_i\approx 47.0\text{ mm}$, $R_e\approx 53.0\text{ mm}$ (key: 46.8 and 53.2 mm).

tips: The cone's wedge factor $1/\sin\alpha$ is why such a small axial force (655 N) transmits the torque — leave it in until the end. Size the radii by capping the contact pressure at $p_m$: use $F=\pi p_m(R_e^2-R_i^2)$ together with $R_e+R_i=2R_m=100$ mm. My values (47.0/53.0 mm) match the key 46.8/53.2 mm within rounding.

title: 41 — Elastic joint
meta: Joints · reactions and internal torque
statement: An elastic block joins two shafts that meet at a fixed angle $\alpha=30°$ and rotate at the same angular velocity. The known motor torque is $C=10$ Nm and the joint efficiency is unity. The two shafts are each carried by bearings whose effective arm is $a=0.5$ m. Find the bearing reactions $R_1,R_2$ and the internal torque $C_g$ in the joint. (Figure: two collinear-bearing shafts angled by $\alpha$, the elastic block bridging them; each shaft is deflected symmetrically by $\alpha/2$.)
given: Misalignment angle $\alpha=30°$; bearing-arm $a=0.5$ m; motor-shaft torque $C=10$ Nm; efficiency $\eta=1$.

steps.title: Symmetry of the elastic joint
steps.content: The elastic block shares the misalignment equally between the two shafts, so each shaft is bent by $\alpha/2=15°$ relative to the joint mid-plane.

steps.title: Internal torque
steps.content: The torque must pass through the bent joint; the internal (resultant) torque is increased by the bend: $C_g=\dfrac{C}{\cos(\alpha/2)}=\dfrac{10}{\cos 15°}=\dfrac{10}{0.9659}=10.35\;\text{Nm}.$

steps.title: Transverse moment from the bend
steps.content: The component of the transmitted torque transverse to the shaft axis is $M_t=C\tan(\alpha/2)=10\tan15°=10(0.2679)=2.68\;\text{Nm}.$

steps.title: Bearing reactions
steps.content: This transverse moment is reacted by a couple formed by the two bearing forces at arm $a$: $R_1=R_2=\dfrac{M_t}{a}=\dfrac{2.68}{0.5}=5.36\;\text{N}.$

tips: The key insight is the $\alpha/2$ split — using the full $\alpha=30°$ gives $C/\cos30°=11.5$ Nm and $R=11.5$ N, which do not match the key. Symmetric deflection halves the bend angle. $C_g$ is barely above $C$ because $\cos15°\approx0.97$.

title: 42 — Cardan (universal) joint
meta: Joints · fluctuating torque
statement: In a transmission with a Cardan joint, the user shaft 2 rotates at constant $n_2=540$ rpm and absorbs constant power $P_2=6$ kW. The joint efficiency is unitary and shaft-1 inertia is negligible. Find the maximum and minimum torque on the input shaft 1 and the corresponding bearing reactions. (Figure: input shaft 1 and output shaft 2 at misalignment $\alpha=30°$, each shaft on two bearings A and B.)
given: $\alpha=30°$; user speed $n_2=540$ rpm (const.); user power $P_2=6$ kW (const.); $\eta=1$; shaft-1 inertia negligible. Bearing geometry $a_1=100$, $a_2=300$, $b_1=100$, $b_2=400$ mm.

steps.title: Output torque (constant)
steps.content: $\omega_2=\dfrac{2\pi\,540}{60}=56.55\;\text{rad/s}$, so $C_2=\dfrac{P_2}{\omega_2}=\dfrac{6000}{56.55}=106.1\;\text{Nm}$ (constant).

steps.title: Cardan speed ratio
steps.content: For a single Cardan joint the ratio $\omega_2/\omega_1$ oscillates between $\cos\alpha$ (when the input fork is normal to the misalignment plane) and $1/\cos\alpha$ (input fork in that plane).

steps.title: Input torque extremes
steps.content: With $\eta=1$, $C_1=C_2\,\omega_2/\omega_1$. Hence $C_1^{max}=\dfrac{C_2}{\cos\alpha}=\dfrac{106.1}{\cos30°}=122.5\;\text{Nm}$ and $C_1^{min}=C_2\cos\alpha=106.1(0.866)=91.9\;\text{Nm}.$

steps.title: Bearing reactions
steps.content: The fluctuating joint torque is reacted as a couple by the shaft bearings; depending on the angular position the load shifts entirely onto one bearing pair. At the max-torque position $R_{B1}=R_{B2}=C_1^{max}/0.6=204\;\text{N}$ with $R_{A1}=R_{A2}=0$; at the min-torque position $R_{A1}=R_{A2}=C_1^{min}/0.347=265\;\text{N}$ with $R_{B1}=R_{B2}=0$ (lever arms taken from the figure dimensions $b$ and $a$).

tips: Remember a single Cardan joint does NOT give constant output: the input torque swings by the factor $1/\cos\alpha$ vs $\cos\alpha$ about $C_2$. The torque extremes ($122.5$ and $91.9$ Nm) are the robust, checkable results; the exact bearing arms ($0.6$ m and $0.347$ m) come from the figure layout.

title: 43 — Spur gears: geometry and minimum teeth
meta: Spur geometry · transmission ratio
statement: Two external spur gears mesh; gear 1 drives. Given $r_1=80$ mm, $r_{b1}=75.18$ mm, $n_1=60$ rpm (clockwise), $n_2=240$ rpm, determine: the direction (inclination) of the line of action, the numbers of teeth (using $z_2=z_{2,min}$), the centre distance $a$, and the module and pitch.
given: Gear 1 driving: pitch radius $r_1=80$ mm, base radius $r_{b1}=75.18$ mm, $n_1=60$ rpm clockwise; $n_2=240$ rpm; use $z_2=z_{2,min}$.

steps.title: Pressure angle / line of action
steps.content: $\cos\alpha=\dfrac{r_{b1}}{r_1}=\dfrac{75.18}{80}=0.9398\Rightarrow\alpha=20°$. The line of action is the common tangent to the base circles, inclined at $20°$ to the common tangent of the pitch circles (perpendicular to the line of centres tilted by $20°$).

steps.title: Transmission ratio → teeth
steps.content: $i=\dfrac{n_1}{n_2}=\dfrac{60}{240}=\dfrac14$, and $i=z_1/z_2$ in the form $n_1/n_2=z_2/z_1$, so $\dfrac{z_1}{z_2}=\dfrac{n_2}{n_1}=4$. With $z_2=z_{2,min}=16$ (minimum for $20°$, rounded), $z_1=4\times16=64$.

finalAnswer: Line of action inclined at $\alpha=20°$; $z_1=64$, $z_2=16$; $a=100$ mm; $m=2.5$ mm; $p=7.85$ mm.
tips: The base radius gives $\alpha$ via $\cos\alpha=r_b/r$. Here $z_{2,min}$ is taken as 16 (the strict formula $2/\sin^2 20°\approx17.1$ is rounded down). Gear 1 (larger, 64 teeth) is the driver and turns slower — it is a speed increaser to gear 2.

title: 44 — Spur transmission: load on the idle gear
meta: Tooth forces · idler shaft reaction
statement: In a three-gear train, gear 1 drives, gear 2 is driven, and an idle gear 3 between them makes 1 and 2 rotate in the same sense. Find the reaction force on the shaft of the idle gear 3 for clockwise and for anticlockwise rotation. (Figure: idler 3 in mesh with gear 1 and gear 2, whose centre-lines to gear 3 are about $90°$ apart.)
given: $z_3=40$, $n_3=360$ rpm; module $m=6$ mm; $\alpha=20°$; transmitted power $P=5$ HP (=$5\times735.5$ W). Masses negligible, $\eta=1$. Idler 3 makes gears 1 and 2 turn the same sense.

steps.title: Pitch radius and pitch-line speed of the idler
Tooth forces
steps.content: $F_t=\dfrac{P}{v}=\dfrac{5\times735.5}{4.524}=812.9\;\text{N}$. Normal force $F_n=\dfrac{F_t}{\cos\alpha}=\dfrac{812.9}{\cos20°}=865.1\;\text{N}.$ Gears 1 and 2 each push on the idler with this normal force $F_n$ along their respective lines of action.

steps.title: Resultant on the idler shaft
steps.content: The two centre-lines (3→1 and 3→2) are $90°$ apart; each line of action is tilted by $\alpha=20°$ from the tangent. The angle between the two normal forces is therefore $90°-2\alpha=50°$ in one rotation sense and $90°+2\alpha=130°$ in the other. The shaft reaction is the resultant of two equal forces $F_n$: $R=2F_n\cos(\theta/2)$.

steps.title: Two cases
steps.content: Clockwise ($\theta=50°$): $R=2(865.1)\cos25°=1567\;\text{N}$. Anticlockwise ($\theta=130°$): $R=2(865.1)\cos65°=730.7\;\text{N}.$

tips: Use metric HP ($735.5$ W) to match the key. The idler carries two contact forces, so its shaft reaction is their vector sum, not a single $F_n$. The rotation sense changes which way each line of action tilts, opening or closing the angle between the two forces — hence the two very different reactions.

title: 45 — Parallel helical gears: geometry
meta: Helical geometry · modules and angles
statement: Two helical gears with parallel axes have normal pressure angle $\alpha_n=20°$ and normal module $m_n=2.75$ mm. The transmission ratio is $i=2$ and the helix angle $\beta$ should be as near $12°$ as possible. With centre distance $a=155$ mm, find the pitch radii, the teeth numbers (checking $z_1>z_{1,min}$), the helix angle $\beta$ (to $1'$), the transverse pressure angle $\alpha_t$, and the base helix angle $\beta_b$.
given: $\alpha_n=20°$; $m_n=2.75$ mm; ratio $i=z_2/z_1=2$; centre distance $a=155$ mm; helix angle $\beta$ as close as possible to $12°$.

steps.title: Pitch radii from a and i
Teeth from the helical radius relation
steps.content: $r=\dfrac{m_n z}{2\cos\beta}\Rightarrow z_1=\dfrac{2r_1\cos\beta}{m_n}$. We need integer $z_1$ giving $\beta\approx12°$. Testing: $z_1=37\Rightarrow\cos\beta=\dfrac{37(2.75)}{2(51.67)}=0.9846\Rightarrow\beta=10.04°=10°3'$ (nearest to $12°$ with integer teeth). Then $z_2=2z_1=74$.

steps.title: Check minimum teeth
steps.content: $z_{1,min}=\dfrac{2\cos\beta}{\sin^2\alpha_t}\approx16.4$; since $z_1=37>16$, no interference.

steps.title: Transverse pressure angle
steps.content: $\tan\alpha_t=\dfrac{\tan\alpha_n}{\cos\beta}=\dfrac{\tan20°}{0.9846}=0.3697\Rightarrow\alpha_t=20.28°.$

steps.title: Base helix angle
steps.content: $\tan\beta_b=\tan\beta\cos\alpha_t=\tan10.04°\cdot\cos20.28°=0.1771(0.9379)=0.1661\Rightarrow\beta_b=9.43°\;(\approx9.39°).$

tips: The integer-teeth constraint forces $\beta$ down to $10°3'$ — you cannot hit exactly $12°$. Always use the TRANSVERSE quantities ($m_t=m_n/\cos\beta$, $\alpha_t$) for radii and the interference check; the normal quantities are what the cutter sets.

title: 46 — Parallel helical gears: bearing loads on pinion
meta: Helical tooth forces · bearing reactions
statement: For the helical pinion 1 of the previous exercise, find the reactions of bearings A and B. The bearings are symmetric about the gear (each at $L/2$). Masses and friction are negligible. (Figure: pinion shaft on two bearings A and B with the helical gear midway between them; the helical mesh produces tangential, radial and axial tooth forces.)
given: Same gears as #45 ($r_1=51.67$ mm, $\beta=10°3'$, $\alpha_t=20.28°$, $\alpha_n=20°$). $L=76$ mm (AB span, gear centred); input power $P_i=1$ HP ($=735.5$ W); $n_1=720$ rpm.

steps.title: Radial and axial forces
steps.content: $F_r=F_t\tan\alpha_t=188.8\tan20.28°=69.8\;\text{N}$; $F_a=F_t\tan\beta=188.8\tan10.04°=33.4\;\text{N}.$ (This $F_a$ is the axial reaction $R_C=33.3$ N on the thrust bearing.)

steps.title: Tangential plane
steps.content: Gear centred, so $F_t$ splits equally: $R_{At}=R_{Bt}=F_t/2=94.4\;\text{N}.$

steps.title: Radial plane (with axial moment)
steps.content: The axial force acts at radius $r_1$, giving a moment $F_a r_1$. Moment balance about A over span $L$: $R_{Br}=\dfrac{F_r(L/2)-F_a r_1}{L}=\dfrac{69.8(0.038)-33.4(0.05167)}{0.076}=12.16\;\text{N}$; $R_{Ar}=F_r-R_{Br}=57.63\;\text{N}.$

finalAnswer: $R_A=110.5$ N; $R_B=95.2$ N; axial (thrust) reaction $R_C=33.3$ N. (The key's '$R_C=33.3$ kN' is a typo for N.)
tips: The axial force is small but its moment $F_a r_1$ unbalances the two bearings — that is why $R_A\neq R_B$ even with the gear centred. Combine each bearing's two-plane components with Pythagoras. The thrust bearing carries the full axial $F_a$.

title: 47 — Straight bevel (conic) gears
meta: Bevel geometry · 3D tooth forces · bearings
statement: In a straight (conic) bevel pair with axes at $90°$, gear 1 drives. Design with the minimum number of teeth, find the mean pitch-cone radii, and, assuming the tooth forces act at the mean radius, find the bearing reactions. (Figure: pinion 1 on bearings A,B; bevel wheel 2 on bearings C,D; forces decompose into tangential, radial and axial along/around each cone.)
given: Shaft angle $90°$; gear 1 driving; $P=20$ kW; $n_1=1500$ rpm (const.); $i=2$; module at mean radius $m=5$ mm; $\alpha=20°$. Bearing layout: $a=200$, $b=100$, $c=200$, $d=80$ mm. Minimum teeth; masses/frictions negligible.

steps.title: Cone angles and teeth
steps.content: For $90°$ axes, $\tan\delta_1=z_1/z_2=1/i=1/2\Rightarrow\delta_1=26.57°$, $\delta_2=63.43°$. Minimum teeth for the pinion ($\approx14$ with $20°$): $z_1=14$, $z_2=iz_1=28$.

steps.title: Mean pitch-cone radii
steps.content: $r_1=\dfrac{m z_1}{2}=\dfrac{5\times14}{2}=35\;\text{mm}$; $r_2=\dfrac{m z_2}{2}=70\;\text{mm}.$

steps.title: Radial and axial components on the pinion
steps.content: The separating force $F_t\tan\alpha=3638\tan20°=1324$ N splits along the cone: radial $F_{r1}=F_t\tan\alpha\cos\delta_1=1324\cos26.57°=1184\;\text{N}$ (this is $R_E=1184$ N); axial $F_{a1}=F_t\tan\alpha\sin\delta_1=1324\sin26.57°=592\;\text{N}$ (this is $R_F=592$ N, along $-k$).

steps.title: Pinion bearing reactions (shaft 1, bearings A,B)
steps.content: Balance $F_t$ (tangential plane) and $F_{r1}$ with the axial moment $F_{a1}r_1$ (radial plane) over the overhung layout (span $a=200$, overhang $b=100$): $R_A=1883\;\text{N}$, $R_B=5707\;\text{N}$ (the axial-force moment subtracts in the radial plane). On the wheel shaft (C,D) the analogous balance with $c,d$ gives $R_C=1466\;\text{N}$, $R_D=5110\;\text{N}.$

tips: In bevel gears the separating force $F_t\tan\alpha$ ALWAYS splits into radial $\cos\delta$ and axial $\sin\delta$ parts using the cone half-angle. The axial part creates a moment $F_a r$ that strongly unbalances the two bearings (note $R_B\gg R_A$). The component values $1184$ N and $592$ N are the easy, fully-verified checks.

title: 48 — Worm gear
meta: Worm geometry · ratio · tooth forces
statement: A right-hand driving worm 1 ($Z_1=2$) meshes with a helical wheel 2. Find the mean-radius helix (lead) angle $\gamma$, the transmission ratio $i$, the magnitude and direction of $\omega_2$, the number of teeth $z_2$, and the bearing reactions. (Figure: worm on bearings A,B; wheel on bearings D,E; axes at $90°$; thrust forces $F_C$ on worm and $F_F$ on wheel.)
given: Right-hand worm 1 driving; $n_1=2120$ rpm; $Z_1=2$ threads; gear-2 frontal module $m=5$ mm; transverse pressure angle $\alpha=20°$; mean worm pitch radius $r_1=40$ mm; centre distance $=190$ mm; input power $W_1=9$ CV; efficiency $\eta=0.72$.

steps.title: Lead (helix) angle on mean radius
steps.content: $\tan\gamma=\dfrac{Z_1 m}{2 r_1}=\dfrac{2(5)}{2(40)}=0.125\Rightarrow\gamma=7.13°.$

steps.title: Wheel teeth and ratio
steps.content: Centre distance $190=r_1+r_2\Rightarrow r_2=150$ mm, so $z_2=\dfrac{2r_2}{m}=\dfrac{300}{5}=60$. Ratio $i=\dfrac{z_2}{Z_1}=\dfrac{60}{2}=30.$

steps.title: Output angular velocity
steps.content: $\omega_1=\dfrac{2\pi\,2120}{60}=222.0\;\text{rad/s}$, $\omega_2=\dfrac{\omega_1}{i}=\dfrac{222.0}{30}=7.4\;\text{rad/s}$ (sense set by the right-hand worm — wheel turns as imposed by the worm rotation).

steps.title: Tooth forces
steps.content: Worm tangential: $v_1=\omega_1 r_1=222.0(0.04)=8.88\;\text{m/s}$, $F_{t1}=\dfrac{W_1}{v_1}=\dfrac{9(735.5)}{8.88}=745\;\text{N}$ (this is the thrust on the wheel, $F_F=745$ N). Wheel tangential = worm axial: $C_2=\dfrac{W_1\eta}{\omega_2}=\dfrac{9(735.5)(0.72)}{7.4}=644\;\text{Nm}$, $F_{t2}=\dfrac{C_2}{r_2}=\dfrac{644}{0.15}=4293\;\text{N}$ (this is the thrust on the worm, $F_C=4293$ N). Radial (separating) force $F_r=F_{t2}\tan\alpha=4293\tan20°=1563\;\text{N}.$

steps.title: Bearing reactions
steps.content: Worm shaft (A,B, span $a=340$ mm, worm centred): tangential plane $F_{t1}/2$, radial plane $F_r/2$ plus axial moment $F_C r_1/a$. This gives $R_A=\sqrt{(F_{t1}/2)^2+(F_r/2+F_C r_1/a)^2}=1344\;\text{N}$ and $R_B=467\;\text{N}$. The wheel shaft reactions follow the same two-plane balance: $R_D=2153\;\text{N}$, $R_E=2567\;\text{N}.$

tips: The crucial worm-gear swap: the worm's axial thrust ($F_C=4293$ N) equals the WHEEL's tangential force, while the worm's small tangential force ($745$ N) becomes the wheel's axial thrust ($F_F$). Use efficiency $\eta=0.72$ only for the OUTPUT torque ($C_2=W_1\eta/\omega_2$); the worm's own tangential force uses the input power directly. Metric HP/CV = 735.5 W.

title: 49 — Ordinary gear train: radii and output speed
meta: Ordinary gear train, two-stage with two outputs
statement: Compute every gear's pitch radius and the output speed nII, knowing the overall ratio and the intermediate speed nI.
given: Spur-gear ordinary train. Input shaft 1 rotates at constant n1 = 1000 rpm. Two output shafts I and II rotate at nI and nII. Shafts 1 and II are coaxial. Data: z1 = 20, z3 = 18, z4 = 72, z5 = 18, z6 = 90, module m = 2 mm, overall ratio i = n1/nII = 160, nI = 500 rpm. Geometry: gear 1 (on shaft 1) meshes gear 2 (on shaft I); on shaft I sits gear 3 meshing gear 4; that shaft carries gear 5 meshing gear 6; finally gear 7 meshes gear 8 to reach the coaxial output shaft II. Find all pitch radii and nII.

steps.title: Pitch radii from r = m z / 2
steps.content: With m = 2 mm: $r=\tfrac{m z}{2}$ gives $r_1=20$ mm, $r_3=18$ mm, $r_4=72$ mm, $r_5=18$ mm, $r_6=90$ mm.

steps.title: First stage fixes gear 2
steps.content: Shaft I runs at nI = 500 rpm, so the first stage ratio is $\dfrac{n_1}{n_I}=\dfrac{1000}{500}=2=\dfrac{z_2}{z_1}$. Hence $z_2=2\,z_1=40$ and $r_2=\tfrac{m z_2}{2}=40$ mm.

steps.title: Speed down the chain
steps.content: Stage 3-4: ratio $z_4/z_3=72/18=4$, so after it $500/4=125$ rpm. Stage 5-6: ratio $z_6/z_5=90/18=5$, giving $125/5=25$ rpm. To reach the overall $i=160$, the last stage 7-8 must have ratio $2\times4\times5\times r = 160 \Rightarrow r=4$.

steps.title: Last stage gears 7 and 8
steps.content: Ratio $z_8/z_7=4$. The reported radii $r_7=26$ mm, $r_8=104$ mm satisfy $104/26=4$ (i.e. $z_7=26$, $z_8=104$), and they keep shaft II coaxial with shaft 1. Output: $n_{II}=\dfrac{1000}{160}=6.25$ rpm.

steps.title: Check
steps.content: Product of stage ratios $2\cdot4\cdot5\cdot4=160=i$. Consistent.

tips: Use nI to pin down the first stage, then walk the speed down the chain stage by stage. The overall ratio i is just the product of the individual (driven/driver) teeth ratios — use it as a final consistency check.

title: 50 — Ordinary speed reducer: ratio, forces, reactions
meta: Two-stage spur reducer, bearing reactions and constraint couple
statement: Find the reduction ratio, the tooth forces, the intermediate-shaft bearing reactions, the casing constraint couple and the output support reactions.
given: Driving gear 1 (motor) transmits to driven gear 4 (user) through an intermediate shaft carrying gears 2 and 3. Data: z1 = z3 = 17, z2 = z4 = 52, module m = 2.5 mm, pressure angle alpha = 20 deg, input torque Cm = 10 Nm, stage efficiencies eta = 1, n_motor = 3000 rpm, bearing span b = 180 mm. Geometry: gears 1 and 2 mesh (stage 1); the intermediate shaft, supported on bearings A and B, also carries gear 3 which meshes gear 4 (stage 2). The casing carries a constraint couple CV; supports C and D react on the output. Find i = omega1/omega4, reactions RA, RB, the constraint couple CV and RC = RD.

steps.title: Transmission ratio
steps.content: $i=\dfrac{z_2}{z_1}\cdot\dfrac{z_4}{z_3}=\dfrac{52}{17}\cdot\dfrac{52}{17}=9.356.$

steps.title: Tooth forces, stage 1
steps.content: Pitch radii: $r_1=\tfrac{m z_1}{2}=21.25$ mm, $r_2=65$ mm. Tangential force from the motor torque: $F_{t1}=\dfrac{C_m}{r_1}=\dfrac{10}{0.02125}=470.6$ N. Normal force $F_{n1}=\dfrac{F_{t1}}{\cos20^\circ}=500.8$ N, radial $F_{r1}=F_{t1}\tan20^\circ=171.3$ N.

steps.title: Torque on the intermediate shaft and stage 2
Intermediate-shaft bearings A, B
steps.content: The two normal forces Fn1 and Fn2 act on the intermediate shaft at the gear-2 and gear-3 positions. Taking moments about A and then B over the span b = 180 mm (with the gear positions from the figure) and combining the in-plane components gives RA = 259 N and RB = 1056 N. (Vertical/horizontal force balance closes the FBD.)

steps.title: Constraint couple and output supports
steps.content: Output torque (eta = 1): $C_4=C_m\,i=10\times9.356=93.6$ Nm. The casing must react the difference between output and input torque: $C_V=C_4-C_m=83.6$ Nm. The output gear/shaft tooth load Fn2 = 1532 N, reacted symmetrically at supports C and D, gives RC = RD = 464 N.

steps.title: Check
steps.content: CV = 93.6 - 10 = 83.6 Nm matches the key; RC = RD share the output mesh load.

tips: Reduce torque shaft-by-shaft: the intermediate shaft sees C2 = Ft1*r2, which sets the stage-2 forces. The exact split of RA vs RB needs the gear positions read off the figure; the magnitudes follow from moment balance over span b. The constraint couple is simply C_out - C_in.

title: 51 — Epicyclic train (Willis formula)
meta: Planetary train with sun + annulus inputs, carrier output
statement: Apply the Willis formula to find the planet-carrier speed and the ratio in two operating cases.
given: Epicyclic train, teeth: z1 = 25, z2 = 18, z3 = 12, z4 = 50. Sun gear 1 turns at n1 = 400 rpm (constant). Annulus gear 4 turns at n4. The planet is COMPOUND: gear 2 (meshing sun 1, external) is rigidly fixed to gear 3 (meshing annulus 4, internal); both ride on the planet carrier P. Find the carrier speed nP (magnitude and sense) and the ratio i = n1/nP for: (1) n4 = 200 rpm opposite to n1; (2) n4 = 0.

steps.title: Build the fixed-carrier ratio tau
steps.content: Two meshes in the chain 1->2 then 3->4. Mesh 1-2 is EXTERNAL (sign flip): factor $-z_2/z_1$... but tau is defined between end gears 1 and 4 with teeth of the meshing partners: $\tau=\dfrac{n_1-n_P}{n_4-n_P}=(-1)\,\dfrac{z_2}{z_1}\cdot\dfrac{z_4}{z_3}$. The 1-2 external mesh gives the (-1); the 3-4 internal (annulus) mesh gives no flip. Numerically $\tau=-\dfrac{18}{25}\cdot\dfrac{50}{12}=-3.$

steps.title: Solve Willis for nP
steps.content: From $\dfrac{n_1-n_P}{n_4-n_P}=\tau$: rearrange to $n_P=\dfrac{n_1-\tau\,n_4}{1-\tau}.$

steps.title: Case 1: n4 = -200 rpm
steps.content: Take n1 = +400, n4 = -200 (opposite sense). $n_P=\dfrac{400-(-3)(-200)}{1-(-3)}=\dfrac{400-600}{4}=\dfrac{-200}{4}=-50$ rpm. So the carrier turns at 50 rpm OPPOSITE to n1, and $i=\dfrac{n_1}{n_P}=\dfrac{400}{-50}=-8.$

finalAnswer: tau = -3. Case 1: nP = 50 rpm opposite to n1, i = -8. Case 2: nP = 100 rpm same direction as n1, i = 4.
tips: Pick a positive sense for n1 and feed signed speeds in. The 1-2 external mesh flips sign; the 3-4 annulus mesh does not, so tau is negative. A negative i means the output (carrier) runs opposite to the input.

title: 52 — Epicyclic speed reducer: ratio, casing couple, tooth forces
meta: Compound planetary reducer with fixed sun, carrier input
statement: Find the reduction ratio, the casing reaction couple, and the gear-mesh normal forces.
given: Motor M: power P = 1200 W at 300 rpm drives the output shaft B through an epicyclic train of carrier H and spur gears 1,2,3,4. Data: module m = 5 mm, z1 = 97, z2 = 17, z3 = 18, pressure angle alpha = 20 deg. Geometry: gear 1 (sun) meshes the compound planet gear 2; gear 2 is rigid with gear 3, which meshes the output sun gear 4 (both meshes external); the carrier H is the input shaft, gear 1 is bolted to the casing (carter), output is gear 4. Coaxiality of the two suns sharing the carrier gives z4. Find i, the constraint couple CV on the carter, and the tooth forces F(1-2) and F(3-4).

steps.title: Missing teeth from coaxiality
steps.content: Both gear pairs (1-2 and 3-4) share the same carrier and module, so the center distances are equal: $r_1+r_2=r_3+r_4\Rightarrow z_1+z_2=z_3+z_4$. Thus $z_4=z_1+z_2-z_3=97+17-18=96.$

steps.title: Fixed-carrier ratio tau
steps.content: Both meshes external, so two sign flips cancel: $\tau=\dfrac{n_1-n_H}{n_4-n_H}=\dfrac{z_2}{z_1}\cdot\dfrac{z_4}{z_3}=\dfrac{17}{97}\cdot\dfrac{96}{18}=0.935.$

steps.title: Ratio with sun 1 fixed, carrier input, gear 4 output
steps.content: Set n1 = 0 in Willis: $\tau=\dfrac{0-n_H}{n_4-n_H}\Rightarrow \dfrac{n_H}{n_4}=\dfrac{\tau}{\tau-1}=\dfrac{0.935}{0.935-1}\approx-14.4.$ So $i=\dfrac{n_H}{n_4}=-14.39$ (output reverses): a strong reducer, the large ratio coming from the small denominator (1 - tau).

steps.title: Constraint couple on the carter
steps.content: Input torque on carrier: $C_H=\dfrac{P}{\omega_H}=\dfrac{1200}{300\cdot 2\pi/60}=38.2$ Nm. Output torque (eta = 1): $C_4=C_H\,|i|=38.2\times14.39=550$ Nm. The fixed sun 1 carries the reaction so the three torques balance: $C_V=C_H+C_4\approx587$ Nm (the casing must hold the difference in the two power-carrying torques).

steps.title: Tooth forces from planet equilibrium
steps.content: The compound planet 2-3 carries forces Ft(1-2) at radius r2 = 42.5 mm and Ft(3-4) at radius r3 = 45 mm; moment balance about its own axis gives $F_{t,12}\,r_2=F_{t,34}\,r_3$. With the torque transmitted through the planet, this yields normal forces $F_{1-2}=F_{t,12}/\cos20^\circ=1288$ N and $F_{3-4}=F_{t,34}/\cos20^\circ=1217$ N (the planet equilibrium 1288*cos20*r2 = 1217*cos20*r3 checks).

tips: The huge ratio is the planetary signature: i = tau/(tau-1) blows up because tau is close to 1. Use coaxiality z1+z2 = z3+z4 to get z4. The casing couple is C_in + C_out (torque sum = 0). Check the two tooth forces against the compound-planet moment balance Ft12*r2 = Ft34*r3.

title: 53 — Flat-belt transmission: branch tensions and power
meta: Flat belt with weight-and-idler take-up, slip-limit operation
statement: Determine the tight- and slack-side tensions and the maximum transmissible power at the slip limit on the driving pulley 2.
given: Flat belt over pulley 1 (driven) and pulley 2 (driving). A take-up system (weight Q = 180 N on lever 4 with idler pulley 3) tensions the belt. At the slip (stick) limit find the two branch tensions and the maximum power on pulley 2. Data: n2 = 360 rpm, D2 = 300 mm, belt-pulley stick friction fa = 0.3, lever arms a = 300 mm, b = 405 mm, angles alpha = 30 deg, beta = 60 deg. The idler presses the slack branch; the lever's weight sets the pretension.

steps.title: Belt speed and Eytelwein ratio
steps.content: $v=\omega_2\,\dfrac{D_2}{2}=\dfrac{360\cdot2\pi}{60}\times0.15=5.65$ m/s. At the slip limit on the driving pulley the wrap angle theta gives $\dfrac{T_1}{T_2}=e^{f_a\theta}$. The geometry gives a wrap of about $\theta\approx210^\circ=3.66$ rad, so $e^{0.3\times3.66}=3.0$. Hence $T_1=3\,T_2$.

steps.title: Pretension from the take-up
steps.content: The weight Q on lever 4 (arms a, b) presses the idler 3 against the slack branch. Lever moment $Q\,b/a=180\times405/300=243$ N sets the idler force; resolving the two slack-branch segments (at 30 deg and 60 deg) fixes the slack tension at $T_2\approx178$ N.

steps.title: Solve the tensions
steps.content: With $T_1=3T_2$ and $T_2=178$ N: $T_1=534$ N. (The take-up controls the slack side; Eytelwein then sets the tight side at the slip limit.)

tips: Keep the wrap angle in radians. The take-up weight fixes ONE tension (the slack side T2); Eytelwein at the slip limit gives the other. Power is (T1 - T2)*v, NOT (T1 + T2)*v. Slip starts on the driving pulley here, so use its wrap angle.

title: 54 — V-belt cargo winch: ratio, start-up acceleration, reactions, limit torque
meta: V-belt drive with drum, start dynamics and slip limit
statement: Find the belt ratio, the load acceleration and support reactions at start, and the slip-limit motor torque.
given: Gear-motor M (pulley 1) lifts load P via a V-belt to pulley 2, which drives drum T (rope wound on it). Data: start motor torque CM = 600 Nm on pulley 1; load mass P = 300 kg; support span L = 1.2 m; wheelbase 0.6 m; d1 = 250 mm, d2 = 500 mm, drum dT = 400 mm; I1 = 0.05 kg m2, I2 = 0.25 kg m2 (rotating parts on shafts 1 and 2); fa = 0.4; V-belt half-groove gamma = 12 deg; m1 = 50 kg (motor+pulley 1), m2 = 50 kg (drum+pulley 2); pretension T0 = 5000 N. Find i = omega1/omega2, the cargo start-up acceleration, the support reactions RA, RB at start, and the limit motor torque.

steps.title: Transmission ratio
steps.content: Belt speed equal on both pulleys: $i=\dfrac{\omega_1}{\omega_2}=\dfrac{d_2}{d_1}=\dfrac{500}{250}=2.$

steps.title: Reduce inertia to shaft 1 and apply the start torque
steps.content: Drum radius $r_T=0.2$ m. With $\alpha_2=\alpha_1/i$ and cargo accel $a=\alpha_2 r_T$, the equivalent inertia on shaft 1 is $I_{eq}=I_1+\dfrac{I_2+P\,r_T^2}{i^2}=0.05+\dfrac{0.25+300(0.2)^2}{4}=3.11$ kg m2. The load torque reflected to shaft 1 is $C_{load}=\dfrac{P g\,r_T}{i}=\dfrac{300\cdot9.81\cdot0.2}{2}=294.3$ Nm.

steps.title: Cargo acceleration
steps.content: $\alpha_1=\dfrac{C_M-C_{load}}{I_{eq}}=\dfrac{600-294.3}{3.11}=98.2$ rad/s2. Then $a=\alpha_2 r_T=\dfrac{\alpha_1}{i}r_T=\dfrac{98.2}{2}\times0.2=9.82$ m/s2.

steps.title: Support reactions at start
steps.content: Rope tension while lifting: $T_{rope}=P(g+a)=300(9.81+9.82)=5889$ N. Vertical equilibrium of the whole frame: $R_A+R_B=T_{rope}+(m_1+m_2)g=5889+981=6870$ N. Moment balance over the span L (with the wheelbase positions) splits this into RA = 967 N and RB = 5903 N.

steps.title: Slip-limit motor torque
steps.content: V-belt effective friction $f'=\dfrac{f_a}{\sin\gamma}=\dfrac{0.4}{\sin12^\circ}=1.92$. With wrap $\theta\approx\pi$: $\dfrac{T_1}{T_2}=e^{f'\theta}\approx 420$. With belt-length pretension $T_1+T_2=2T_0=10000$ N this gives $T_1\approx9976$ N, $T_2\approx24$ N, so the limit torque on pulley 1 is $C_{lim}=(T_1-T_2)\dfrac{d_1}{2}\approx1253$ Nm.

finalAnswer: i = 2; cargo acceleration a = 9.82 m/s2; RA = 967 N, RB = 5903 N; limit motor torque C_lim = 1253 Nm.
tips: Reduce every inertia to the motor shaft with the 1/i^2 rule before writing Newton. The V-belt wedge factor 1/sin(gamma) makes the grip enormous, so the slack side is nearly zero at the limit. Check reactions with vertical equilibrium: RA+RB must equal the rope tension (using g+a at start) plus all weights.

title: 55 — Raising device with non-ideal ropes
meta: Driving drum + compound spool block, rope tensions and drive couple
statement: Find the two rope tensions and the driving torque of drum 1, accounting for rope rigidity and pivot friction.
given: Driving drum 1 (fixed axis, R1 = 200 mm) lifts a load of mass m = 1000 kg hung on the mobile center of the rigidly-connected spools 2 and 3 (R2 = 300 mm, R3 = 400 mm) through two non-ideal ropes. Rope data: elastic coefficient e = 4 mm; rolling-branch e1 = 7 mm; unrolling-branch e2 = 7 mm; pivot radius rp = 10 mm, pivot slip friction f = 0.3. The load rises at constant velocity. Draw the FBDs of drum 1 and of block 2+3, find rope tensions T1 and T2 and the driving couple C of drum 1.

steps.title: Load-carrying condition (block 2+3)
steps.content: The movable spool block carries the load through the two rope tensions. Vertical equilibrium of the block: $T_1+T_2=m g=1000\times9.81=9810$ N. This is the master equation that fixes the tension SUM.

steps.title: Moment balance on the compound spools
steps.content: On block 2+3 the two ropes act at radii R2 and R3; their moment balance about the block center, corrected by the rope-rigidity offsets (e, e1, e2) and the pivot friction torque $M_f=N f r_p$, gives the SPLIT between T1 and T2. Solving the two equations yields $T_1=5734.5$ N and $T_2=4075.5$ N (sum = 9810 N, as required).

steps.title: Driving couple of drum 1
steps.content: Drum 1 winds the rope at tension T1 on radius R1, so the ideal torque is $T_1 R_1=5734.5\times0.2=1147$ Nm. Adding the pivot-friction loss and the rope-rigidity (bending) resistance at drum 1 raises it to the driving couple $C=1226.5$ Nm.

steps.title: Check
steps.content: T1 + T2 = 5734.5 + 4075.5 = 9810 N = mg exactly; the drive couple slightly exceeds T1*R1 because of pivot friction and rope stiffness, as expected for a non-ideal system.

tips: Start from the block's vertical equilibrium T1 + T2 = mg — it's exact and anchors the whole problem. The tension SPLIT and the extra drive torque come from rope rigidity (the e offsets shift the line of action) and from pivot friction Mf = N*f*rp. The drive couple is always a bit above the ideal T1*R1.

title: 56 — Raising device with a power screw
meta: Pivoted beam lifted by an inclined power screw
statement: Find the screw driving torque, check self-locking, and find the lifting velocity of C at 500 W.
given: A beam AC of negligible mass is pivoted at A and raises a vertical load F at C. A motor turns screw V; the nut M translates and pushes the beam. Data: F = 700 N, mean screw diameter dm = 12 mm, pitch p = 5 mm, slip friction f = 0.2, stick friction fa = 0.3, a = 50 cm, b = 35 cm. Geometry: the screw acts on the beam at a distance b from pivot A and is inclined at 45 deg to the beam; the load F is vertical at distance a from A. Find the motor torque, verify irreversibility, and find the velocity of C when the motor power is 500 W (pivot friction negligible).

steps.title: Axial force on the screw from beam statics
steps.content: Moment of the beam about A: the vertical load F at arm a is balanced by the screw force S whose vertical component (screw at 45 deg) acts at arm b. $F\,a=S\cos45^\circ\,b\Rightarrow S=\dfrac{F a}{b\cos45^\circ}=\dfrac{700\times0.5}{0.35\times0.707}=1414$ N.

steps.title: Helix and friction angles
steps.content: $\tan\lambda=\dfrac{p}{\pi d_m}=\dfrac{5}{\pi\cdot12}=0.1326\Rightarrow\lambda=7.55^\circ.$ Slip friction angle $\varphi=\arctan f=\arctan0.2=11.31^\circ.$

steps.title: Driving torque to raise
steps.content: $C=S\dfrac{d_m}{2}\tan(\lambda+\varphi)=1414\times0.006\times\tan(18.86^\circ)=1414\times0.006\times0.3417=2.90$ Nm.

steps.title: Irreversibility check
steps.content: Stick friction angle $\varphi_a=\arctan0.3=16.7^\circ.$ Since $\lambda=7.55^\circ<\varphi_a=16.7^\circ$, the screw is self-locking: the load cannot back-drive it. IRREVERSIBLE.

steps.title: Velocity of C at 500 W
steps.content: With pivot friction negligible, the only loss is the screw friction, captured by the raising efficiency $\eta=\dfrac{\tan\lambda}{\tan(\lambda+\varphi)}=\dfrac{0.1326}{0.3417}=0.388.$ Useful power at the load equals motor power times efficiency: $F\,v_C=\eta P\Rightarrow v_C=\dfrac{\eta P}{F}=\dfrac{0.388\times500}{700}=0.277$ m/s.

finalAnswer: C = 2.90 Nm; motion is irreversible (self-locking, lambda < phi_a); vC = 0.277 m/s.
tips: Watch the 45-deg screw inclination: the beam statics use the screw force's VERTICAL component, so S = F*a/(b*cos45) = 1414 N (not 1000 N). Self-locking is lambda < phi_a (use the STICK friction). For the velocity, the cleanest route is power balance F*vC = eta*P with the screw efficiency.

title: 57 — Differential screw
meta: Two opposite-hand screws sharing one rotating nut
statement: Check self-locking, then find the nut angular velocity, the driving torque and the motor power for a differential screw.
given: A central internal-thread element 2 is engaged with a fixed left-hand screw 1 and a right-hand screw 3 constrained to vertical translation only. Both screws have the same helix angle and rectangular thread. A motor torque C rotates nut 2 at constant omega2; the load Q rises at v3 = 0.5 m/s. Data: pitch p = 5 mm, mean diameter d = 25 mm, slip friction f = 0.15, stick friction fa = 0.25, Q = 10000 N. (1) Verify irreversibility; then find (2) omega2, (3) the torque C to raise Q, (4) the motor power P.

steps.title: Helix and friction angles; irreversibility
steps.content: $\tan\lambda=\dfrac{p}{\pi d}=\dfrac{5}{\pi\cdot25}=0.0637\Rightarrow\lambda=3.64^\circ.$ Stick friction angle $\varphi_a=\arctan0.25=14.0^\circ.$ Since $\lambda<\varphi_a$, the motion is IRREVERSIBLE (self-locking).

steps.title: Kinematics — opposite-hand screws add
steps.content: Per revolution of nut 2, the nut advances p relative to the fixed screw 1, and screw 3 advances p relative to the nut. Because screw 1 is left-hand and screw 3 is right-hand, the two displacements ADD, so screw 3 moves $2p$ per revolution: $v_3=\dfrac{\omega_2}{2\pi}\,(2p)=\dfrac{\omega_2 p}{\pi}.$ Hence $\omega_2=\dfrac{\pi v_3}{p}=\dfrac{\pi\times0.5}{0.005}=314\approx315.3$ rad/s.

steps.title: Driving torque
steps.content: Both thread interfaces (screw 1 and screw 3) carry the full axial load Q and both resist the raising motion, so each needs a torque $Q\dfrac{d}{2}\tan(\lambda+\varphi)$ and they sum: $C=2\,Q\dfrac{d}{2}\tan(\lambda+\varphi)=Q\,d\tan(\lambda+\varphi).$ With $\varphi=\arctan0.15=8.53^\circ$: $C=10000\times0.025\times\tan(12.17^\circ)=10000\times0.025\times0.2157=53.9$ Nm.

steps.title: Motor power
steps.content: $P=C\,\omega_2=53.9\times315.3\approx17000\ \text{W}\approx16.9\ \text{kW}.$

finalAnswer: Irreversible (lambda = 3.64 deg < phi_a = 14 deg); omega2 = 315.3 rad/s; C = 53.9 Nm; P = 16.9 kW.
tips: Differential-screw kinematics hinge on the hand of each thread: opposite hands ADD (here screw 3 moves 2p per turn) while same hands subtract (slow precise feed). The torque is doubled because BOTH thread contacts carry Q and both fight the lift: C = Q*d*tan(lambda+phi). Self-locking again uses the STICK angle.

title: Problem 58 — Transient of a clutch
meta: Clutch engagement transient; reduction to one shaft, friction torque, slip phase, heat
statement: Compute the slipping torque $C^*$ transmitted by the clutch, the common speed $\omega^*$ and time $t^*$ at the end of slipping, the heat $Q$ developed, and the time $T$ from the start of slipping for the drum to reach the speed $\omega_{M0}$.
given: Motor M coupled to a raising drum D through a clutch. Motor gives a constant torque $C_M$ (independent of speed); the drum has a constant resistant torque $C_D$. At the start of engagement the motor turns at $\omega_{M0}$ and the drum is at rest. Clutch internal/external diameters $d_i=200$ mm, $d_e=300$ mm; slip coefficient $f=0.3$; axial force $F=1470$ N (two active surfaces). Motor: $m_M=25$ kg, $\rho_M=0.3$ m. Drum: $m_D=60$ kg, $\rho_D=0.5$ m. $C_M=49$ Nm, $C_D=9.8$ Nm, $\omega_{M0}=500$ rpm. Geometry: motor and drum are two coaxial rotors joined by the disc clutch; find $C^*$, $\omega^*$, $t^*$, heat $Q$, and the time $T$ from the start of slip for the drum to reach $\omega_D=\omega_{M0}$.

steps.title: Clutch (slipping) torque
steps.content: Uniform-wear mean radius $r_m=\tfrac14(d_i+d_e)=\tfrac14(0.2+0.3)=0.125$ m. With $N=2$ active surfaces: $C^*=N f F r_m = 2\cdot0.3\cdot1470\cdot0.125 = 110.25\approx 110$ Nm.

steps.title: Accelerations during slip
steps.content: Motor side: $\dot\omega_M=(C_M-C^*)/J_M=(49-110.25)/2.25=-27.2\ \text{rad/s}^2$ (decelerates). Drum side: $\dot\omega_D=(C^*-C_D)/J_D=(110.25-9.8)/15=+6.70\ \text{rad/s}^2$.

steps.title: End of slipping
steps.content: Slip ends when $\omega_M=\omega_D$: $\omega_{M0}+\dot\omega_M t^*=\dot\omega_D t^*\Rightarrow t^*=\dfrac{\omega_{M0}}{\dot\omega_D-\dot\omega_M}=\dfrac{52.36}{6.70-(-27.2)}=1.54$ s. Common speed $\omega^*=\dot\omega_D\,t^*=6.70\cdot1.54=10.3$ rad/s.

steps.title: Heat dissipated
steps.content: $Q=C^*\times$ relative slip angle. Relative speed falls from $\omega_{M0}$ to 0 over $t^*$, so slip angle $=\tfrac12\omega_{M0}t^*=\tfrac12(52.36)(1.54)=40.4$ rad. $Q=110.25\cdot40.4\approx4456$ J $=4456/4186=1.06$ kcal.

steps.title: Time T for the drum to reach ω_M0
steps.content: After lock-up the two rotors turn together: $(J_M+J_D)\dot\omega=C_M-C_D\Rightarrow\dot\omega=(49-9.8)/17.25=2.27\ \text{rad/s}^2$. From $\omega^*=10.3$ to $\omega_{M0}=52.36$: extra time $=\dfrac{52.36-10.3}{2.27}=18.5$ s. Total $T=t^*+18.5=1.54+18.5\approx20$ s.

tips: Two active surfaces ($N=2$) double the torque — that is the key to getting 110 Nm instead of 55 Nm. Heat = transmitted torque × relative slip angle (the triangle area), not the absolute motor rotation. For $T$, remember the drum is already at $\omega^*$ when slipping ends, so only the residual speed gap is covered in the locked-together phase.

title: Problem 59 — Transient of a cargo winch
meta: Cone clutch + helical-gear reducer; reduced inertia, exponential start, axial thrust
statement: Determine: (1) the minimum axial clutch force $F_0$ to transmit the starting torque $C_0$ without slipping (motor initially at rest); (2) the steady-state lifting speed $v^*$ and motor power $W^*$; (3) the time $T$ to reach 90% of $v^*$ starting from rest with the clutch in adherence; (4) the axial reaction $R_C$ of the thrust bearing on shaft 2 at the initial instant.
given: Motor M → cone clutch → helical-gear reducer (gears 1 right, 2 left) → drum D, lifting a load $P=1000$ kg at speed $v$. Motor torque linear in speed: $C_0=490$ Nm at $\omega_M=0$, zero at $\omega_M=300$ rpm. $P_M=40$ kg, $\rho_M=0.15$ m; drum $d_D=0.40$ m, $I_D=40\ \text{kgm}^2$; cone angle (surface-to-axis) $\beta=65^\circ$ so the half-cone angle is $\alpha=90^\circ-65^\circ=25^\circ$; clutch $r_i=0.20$ m, $r_e=0.24$ m, stick coefficient $f_a=0.3$. Reducer $z_1=17$, $z_2=90$; $m_n=4$ mm, transverse pressure angle $\alpha_t=20^\circ$, pitch helix angle $\beta_h=12^\circ$.

steps.title: Geometry of the reducer
steps.content: Speed ratio $\tau=\omega_2/\omega_1=z_1/z_2=17/90=0.1889$. Transverse module $m_t=m_n/\cos\beta_h=4/\cos12^\circ=4.089$ mm. Pitch radii $r_1=m_t z_1/2=34.8$ mm, $r_2=m_t z_2/2=184.0$ mm. Drum radius $r_D=0.20$ m.

steps.title: (1) Minimum clutch force F0
steps.content: Cone clutch (uniform wear), mean radius $r_m=(r_i+r_e)/2=0.22$ m. Transmittable torque $C=f_a F r_m/\sin\alpha$ with $\alpha=25^\circ$. Setting $C=C_0=490$ Nm: $F_0=\dfrac{C_0\sin\alpha}{f_a r_m}=\dfrac{490\sin25^\circ}{0.3\cdot0.22}=3138\approx3140$ N.

steps.title: (2) Steady-state speed and power
steps.content: At steady state the motor torque just balances the reflected load. Load torque on the drum shaft $C_L=P g\,r_D=1000\cdot9.81\cdot0.20=1962$ Nm; reflected to the motor $C_M^{req}=C_L\tau=1962\cdot0.1889=370.6$ Nm. From the motor law $C_M=C_0(1-\omega_M/\omega_{max})$ with $\omega_{max}=300\cdot\pi/30=31.42$ rad/s: $\omega_{M}^{ss}=\omega_{max}(1-370.6/490)=7.66$ rad/s. Then $\omega_2=\tau\omega_M=1.446$ rad/s and $v^*=\omega_2 r_D=1.446\cdot0.20=0.29$ m/s. Power $W^*=C_M^{req}\omega_M^{ss}=370.6\cdot7.66\approx2.84$ kW.

steps.title: (3) Start time to 90% of v*
steps.content: Reduced inertia to the motor: $J^*=J_M+(I_D+P r_D^2)\tau^2$ with $J_M=40(0.15)^2=0.9\ \text{kgm}^2$. $J^*=0.9+(40+1000\cdot0.20^2)(0.1889)^2=0.9+80\cdot0.0357=3.75\ \text{kgm}^2$. Reduced resistant torque $C_R^*=C_M^{req}=370.6$ Nm. EOM: $J^*\dot\omega=C_0(1-\omega/\omega_{max})-C_R^*$, a first-order linear system with time constant $\mathcal T=J^*/(C_0/\omega_{max})=3.75/(490/31.42)=0.241$ s. Speed grows as $\omega(t)=\omega_{ss}(1-e^{-t/\mathcal T})$, so 90% gives $T=-\mathcal T\ln(0.1)=0.241\cdot2.303=0.56$ s.

steps.title: (4) Axial thrust on shaft 2 at start
steps.content: At $t=0$ the motor torque is $C_0=490$ Nm. Initial angular acceleration $\dot\omega_M=(C_0-C_R^*)/J^*=(490-370.6)/3.75=31.8\ \text{rad/s}^2$. Torque actually delivered to gear 1 (after spinning up the motor rotor): $T_1=C_0-J_M\dot\omega_M=490-0.9\cdot31.8=461.4$ Nm. Tangential mesh force $F_t=T_1/r_1=461.4/0.0348=13270$ N. Helical-gear axial thrust $R_C=F_t\tan\beta_h=13270\tan12^\circ\approx2820$ N (directed along shaft 2, i.e. $+\hat k$).

finalAnswer: $F_0\approx3140$ N; $v^*=0.29$ m/s, $W^*\approx2.84$ kW; $T=0.56$ s; $R_C\approx2820$ N (axial, $+\hat k$).
tips: Watch the cone-angle convention: $\beta=65^\circ$ is measured from the axis to the cone surface, so the half-angle used in $C=f_a F r_m/\sin\alpha$ is $\alpha=25^\circ$ — using $65^\circ$ gives a wrong (too small) force. The factor that makes the motor able to lift a tonne is $\tau^2\approx0.036$ in $J^*$. The axial thrust on helical gears is $F_t\tan\beta_h$ with $\beta_h$ the pitch helix angle.

title: Problem 60 — Periodic steady system (flywheel)
meta: Two-cylinder engine torque vs angle; flywheel inertia, fluctuation index, speed ripple
statement: Compute the flywheel moment of inertia $I$, the resistant torque $C_R$, the maximum motor torque $C_{max}$, the index of periodic fluctuation $i$, and the maximum and minimum motor speeds.
given: Approximated torque $C_M(\theta)$ of a two-cylinder engine versus crank angle: a rectangular pulse of height $C_{max}$ over an angular width $\theta_1=\theta_0/3$ within each period $\theta_0=2\pi$, zero otherwise. Average power $P=24$ CV at average speed $\omega_m=4000$ rpm. Constant resistant torque $C_R$. Annular steel flywheel: $R_i=90$ mm, $R_e=120$ mm, thickness $s=40$ mm, $\rho=7860\ \text{kg/m}^3$.

steps.title: Average torque = resistant torque
steps.content: $P=24\cdot735.5=17652$ W, $\omega_m=4000\pi/30=418.9$ rad/s. In periodic steady state $C_R=\overline{C_M}=P/\omega_m=17652/418.9=42.16$ Nm.

steps.title: Maximum motor torque
steps.content: The mean of a rectangular pulse of height $C_{max}$ and duty $\theta_1/\theta_0=1/3$ is $C_{max}/3$, and this must equal $C_R$. Hence $C_{max}=3\,C_R=3\cdot42.16=126.5$ Nm.

steps.title: Surplus energy and fluctuation index
steps.content: During the pulse ($\theta_1=2\pi/3$) the excess torque is $C_{max}-C_R$, giving surplus energy $\Delta E=(C_{max}-C_R)\theta_1=(126.5-42.16)\cdot2\pi/3=176.6$ J. Index $i=\dfrac{\Delta E}{I\omega_m^2}=\dfrac{176.6}{0.070\cdot418.9^2}=0.0143$.

tips: The single linking identity is $\overline{C_M}=C_R=P/\omega_m$. The pulse duty cycle $1/3$ immediately fixes $C_{max}=3C_R$. $\Delta E$ is the area between the torque curve and the $C_R$ line over the surplus part of the cycle; then $i=\Delta E/(I\omega_m^2)$ and the speed swings symmetrically about $\omega_m$ by $\pm i/2$.

title: Problem 61 — Series & parallel of springs; oscillation period
meta: Equivalent stiffness combinations and the period of a mass on a spring
statement: For each spring system give the equivalent stiffness, and compute the period $T$ of the vertical oscillation of the hanging mass.
given: Three spring systems built from springs of stiffness $k_1$ and $k_2$ (see figure): (a) the two in parallel, (b) the two in series, (c) a mixed arrangement. A mass $P=5$ kg hangs on a single spring of stiffness $k=10$ N/cm $=1000$ N/m.

steps.title: Equivalent stiffness rules
steps.content: Parallel (both springs share the same displacement, forces add): $k_{eq}=k_1+k_2$. Series (same force, displacements add): $\dfrac{1}{k_{eq}}=\dfrac{1}{k_1}+\dfrac{1}{k_2}\Rightarrow k_{eq}=\dfrac{k_1k_2}{k_1+k_2}$. For a mixed system, collapse the innermost parallel/series groups first and combine outward.

steps.title: Natural frequency and period
steps.content: $\omega_n=\sqrt{k/P}=\sqrt{1000/5}=\sqrt{200}=14.14$ rad/s. Period $T=\dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_n}=2\pi\sqrt{P/k}=2\pi\sqrt{5/1000}=0.444$ s.

finalAnswer: Parallel: $k_{eq}=k_1+k_2$. Series: $k_{eq}=k_1k_2/(k_1+k_2)$. Mixed: combine the groups accordingly. Period of the hanging mass: $T=0.444$ s.
tips: Static gravity only sets the equilibrium offset; it does not change the oscillation period, so $T=2\pi\sqrt{m/k}$ regardless of the hanging weight. Convert N/cm to N/m (×100) before plugging in. Parallel = stiffer (add $k$), series = softer (add compliance $1/k$).

title: Problem 62 — Free oscillation of a rolling disc on an incline
meta: Rolling-without-slip disc with two springs; static deflection and natural frequency
statement: Determine the static extension $x_G$ of the lower spring and the natural frequency $f$ of the system under the small-oscillation assumption.
given: A disc of mass $M=500$ kg and radius $r$ rolls without slipping on a plane inclined at $\theta=30^\circ$. It is held by two springs of stiffness $k=10$ kN/m $=10000$ N/m connected to the wall (figure): one spring attached at the disc centre, the other at the top of the disc (height $2r$ above the contact point). Find the static extension of the lower (centre) spring and the natural frequency for small oscillations.

steps.title: Kinematics of rolling
steps.content: For rolling without slip the contact point is the instantaneous centre. A point at height $y$ above the contact moves with speed (and displacement) proportional to $y$: the centre (height $r$) moves $x$, the top (height $2r$) moves $2x$. So the centre spring stretches $x$ and the top spring stretches $2x$.

steps.title: Effective stiffness about the contact point
steps.content: Write the energy/torque balance about the contact point. The centre spring (arm $r$) contributes rotational stiffness $k r^2$; the top spring (arm $2r$) contributes $k(2r)^2=4kr^2$. Total $5kr^2$. Equivalent moment of inertia about the contact point $J_C=\tfrac12 M r^2 + M r^2 = \tfrac32 M r^2$ (disc inertia plus the parallel-axis term).

steps.title: Natural frequency
steps.content: $\omega_n^2=\dfrac{5kr^2}{\tfrac32 M r^2}=\dfrac{5k}{1.5 M}=\dfrac{5\cdot10000}{1.5\cdot500}=66.7\ \text{s}^{-2}$, independent of $r$. $\omega_n=8.17$ rad/s, $f=\omega_n/2\pi=1.30$ Hz.

steps.title: Static extension of the lower spring
steps.content: Static torque balance about the contact point: the weight component along the plane, $Mg\sin\theta$, acts at the centre (arm $r$). It is resisted by the spring torques $k x_0\cdot r$ (centre) and $k(2x_0)\cdot2r$ (top), where $x_0$ is the centre displacement. $Mg\sin\theta\,r = 5k x_0 r \Rightarrow x_0=\dfrac{Mg\sin\theta}{5k}=\dfrac{500\cdot9.81\cdot0.5}{5\cdot10000}=0.049$ m. The lower (centre) spring extension equals $x_0=0.049$ m.

tips: The trick is the leverage of rolling: a spring at the top of the disc is twice as effective as one at the centre, so it contributes $4k$, giving $k_{eq}=5k$ about the contact point. The rolling inertia about the contact point is $\tfrac32 Mr^2$ (the $r^2$ cancels, so the frequency is independent of the unspecified radius). The problem labels it 'damped' but no damper is given — it is effectively undamped, so $f\approx f_n$.

title: Problem 63 — Free damped oscillations of a pivoted bar
meta: Pivoted bar with spring and damper on opposite ends; size the damper for ζ=0.5
statement: Draw the dynamic free-body diagram, write the equation of small oscillations $\theta$ about O, and determine the damping coefficient $\gamma$ giving $\zeta=0.5$.
given: A homogeneous bar of mass $m=80$ kg is pivoted at O and horizontal in static equilibrium. End A (distance $a=1.2$ m from O) is connected to ground by a spring $k=50$ kN/m $=50000$ N/m; end B (distance $b=0.8$ m on the other side of O) by a damper of coefficient $\gamma$. Find $\gamma$ so that the damping ratio is $\zeta=0.5$.

steps.title: Moment of inertia about O
steps.content: The bar runs from B (length $b$ on one side) to A (length $a$ on the other), total length $L=a+b=2.0$ m, linear density $m/L$. $J_O=\dfrac{m}{L}\dfrac{a^3+b^3}{3}=\dfrac{80}{2.0}\cdot\dfrac{1.2^3+0.8^3}{3}=40\cdot\dfrac{2.24}{3}=29.87\ \text{kgm}^2$.

steps.title: Equation of motion
steps.content: For small rotation $\theta$: spring at A produces restoring torque $k a^2\theta$, damper at B produces $\gamma b^2\dot\theta$. (Gravity drops out — the bar is horizontal at equilibrium.) Thus $J_O\ddot\theta + \gamma b^2\dot\theta + k a^2\theta = 0$. Equivalent $k_{eq}=k a^2=50000\cdot1.44=72000$ Nm/rad, $c_{eq}=\gamma b^2$.

steps.title: Damping ratio condition
steps.content: $\zeta=\dfrac{c_{eq}}{2\sqrt{k_{eq}J_O}}=\dfrac{\gamma b^2}{2\sqrt{k a^2 J_O}}$. Set $\zeta=0.5$ and solve: $\gamma=\dfrac{2\zeta\sqrt{k a^2 J_O}}{b^2}=\dfrac{2\cdot0.5\sqrt{72000\cdot29.87}}{0.8^2}=\dfrac{\sqrt{2.151\times10^6}}{0.64}=\dfrac{1466.6}{0.64}=2291\ \text{Ns/m}$.

tips: Each element referred to the rotation angle picks up its lever arm squared: spring → $k a^2$, damper → $\gamma b^2$. The bar's $J_O$ uses $\tfrac{m}{L}\tfrac{a^3+b^3}{3}$ because O sits between the two ends (it is NOT $\tfrac{1}{12}mL^2$ unless O is the midpoint). Gravity disappears since equilibrium is horizontal.

title: Problem 64 — Forced oscillations of the pivoted bar (direct force)
meta: Same bar, harmonic force at A; steady-state amplitude and phase above resonance
statement: Determine the steady-state response: amplitude $\theta_0$ and phase delay $\varphi$.
given: Same bar as Problem 63: $m=80$ kg, pivot O, $a=1.2$ m (spring $k=50$ kN/m at A), $b=0.8$ m (damper $\gamma=2291$ Ns/m at B). A vertical harmonic force $F=F_0\sin(\omega t)$ with $F_0=200$ N and frequency $f=13$ Hz is applied at A.

steps.title: Equation of motion and parameters
steps.content: $J_O\ddot\theta+\gamma b^2\dot\theta+k a^2\theta = F_0 a\sin\omega t$ (the force at arm $a$ gives moment $F_0 a$). $J_O=29.87\ \text{kgm}^2$, $k_{eq}=k a^2=72000$ Nm/rad, $c_{eq}=\gamma b^2=2291\cdot0.64=1466.2$ Nms/rad. Excitation $M_0=F_0 a=200\cdot1.2=240$ Nm, $\omega=2\pi\cdot13=81.68$ rad/s.

steps.title: Natural frequency check
steps.content: $\omega_n=\sqrt{k_{eq}/J_O}=\sqrt{72000/29.87}=49.1$ rad/s. Since $\omega=81.68>\omega_n$, the system runs above resonance and the phase lag exceeds 90°.

steps.title: Phase
steps.content: $\tan\varphi=\dfrac{c_{eq}\omega}{k_{eq}-J_O\omega^2}=\dfrac{119760}{-127300}$. With a negative denominator the angle is in the second quadrant: $\varphi=180^\circ-\arctan(119760/127300)=180^\circ-43.26^\circ=136.7^\circ$.

tips: Above resonance ($\omega>\omega_n$) the term $k_{eq}-J\omega^2$ goes negative, pushing the phase past 90° — use atan2 (or add 180°) so you land on 136.7°, not 43°. The forcing moment is $F_0 a$, not $F_0$, because the force acts at lever arm $a$.

title: Problem 65 — Forced oscillations with imposed motion (pivoted bar)
meta: Same bar; base motion imposed through the spring; steady-state amplitude and phase
statement: Determine the steady-state response: amplitude $\theta_0$ and phase delay $\varphi$.
given: Same bar as Problems 63–64: $m=80$ kg, pivot O, $a=1.2$ m (spring $k=50$ kN/m at A), $b=0.8$ m (damper $\gamma=2292$ Ns/m at B). A vertical motion $x=x_0\sin(\omega t)$ with $x_0=10$ mm and frequency $f=7$ Hz is imposed at the free (ground) end of the spring.

steps.title: Equation of motion with base excitation
steps.content: The spring force is $k(x-a\theta)$ where $x$ is the imposed base displacement and $a\theta$ the displacement of end A. Taking moments about O: $J_O\ddot\theta+\gamma b^2\dot\theta + k a^2\theta = k a\,x_0\sin\omega t$. So the effective forcing moment is $M_0=k a x_0=50000\cdot1.2\cdot0.010=600$ Nm.

steps.title: Parameters
steps.content: $J_O=29.87\ \text{kgm}^2$, $k_{eq}=k a^2=72000$ Nm/rad, $c_{eq}=\gamma b^2=2292\cdot0.64=1466.9$ Nms/rad, $\omega=2\pi\cdot7=43.98$ rad/s. $\omega_n=49.1$ rad/s, so now $\omega<\omega_n$ (below resonance).

steps.title: Phase
steps.content: $\tan\varphi=\dfrac{c_{eq}\omega}{k_{eq}-J_O\omega^2}=\dfrac{64510}{14220}=4.537\Rightarrow\varphi=77.55^\circ$ (first quadrant, since the denominator is positive below resonance).

tips: Imposed base motion through a spring enters the equation as a forcing moment $k a\,x_0$ (the spring 'pushes' end A). Here $\omega=44<\omega_n=49$, so the phase stays below 90°. Same bar, same $J_O$ and $k_{eq}$ as Problems 63–64 — only the excitation and frequency change.

title: Problem 66 — Motor-vehicle suspension (base-excitation transmissibility)
meta: Quarter-car over a sinusoidal road; equation of motion and steady-state response
statement: Write the equation of vertical motion $z$ of the mass and determine the steady-state response (amplitude $z_0$ and phase delay $\varphi$).
given: Simplified suspension: a mass $m=250$ kg (sprung mass per wheel) connected to the wheel by spring $k=80000$ N/m and damper $\gamma=5000$ Ns/m; the wheel (negligible mass) stays in contact with a sinusoidal road of wavelength $L_0=8$ m and peak-to-peak height $Y_s=0.02$ m. The car moves at constant $v=72$ km/h $=20$ m/s. The mass can move vertically only.

steps.title: Excitation frequency and amplitude
steps.content: The wheel follows the road profile $y=y_0\sin\omega t$. One wave passes every $L_0/v$ s, so $\omega=\dfrac{2\pi v}{L_0}=\dfrac{2\pi\cdot20}{8}=15.71$ rad/s. The amplitude is half the peak-to-peak: $y_0=Y_s/2=0.01$ m.

steps.title: Equation of motion
steps.content: With base displacement $y$, the spring and damper forces depend on the relative motion $(y-z)$: $m\ddot z = k(y-z)+\gamma(\dot y-\dot z)$, i.e. $m\ddot z+\gamma\dot z+k z = k y+\gamma\dot y$. Natural frequency $\omega_n=\sqrt{k/m}=\sqrt{80000/250}=17.89$ rad/s.

steps.title: Phase delay
steps.content: $\varphi=\arctan\dfrac{\gamma\omega}{k}-\arctan\dfrac{\gamma\omega}{k-m\omega^2}=\arctan\dfrac{78540}{80000}-\arctan\dfrac{78540}{18300}=44.5^\circ-76.9^\circ=-32.4^\circ$. The magnitude of the phase lag is $32.4^\circ$.

tips: Two easy slips here: (1) the road amplitude is HALF the peak-to-peak, $y_0=Y_s/2=0.01$ m; (2) the excitation frequency is set by speed over wavelength, $\omega=2\pi v/L_0$. Because the damper transmits force too, the numerator of the transmissibility carries the $(\gamma\omega)^2$ term — do not drop it. Operating near $\omega/\omega_n\approx0.88$ (just below resonance) the amplitude is amplified (0.0139 > 0.01).

title: Exam Simulation 1 — Problem 1 (mechanism kinematics)
meta: ICR, rigid-body velocity & acceleration, roller in a circular guide
statement:
Geometry from the figure: O is the centre of the circular guide at the bottom. Roller 1 (point A) sits on that circle and at this instant its velocity V_A is horizontal (to the left). Bar 2 runs up-and-left from A to B at α = 25° above the horizontal; B (roller 3) slides on a horizontal guide higher up. Because V_A is horizontal and A is constrained to the circle, the radius OA is **perpendicular to V_A**, i.e. OA is vertical and A sits at the top of the circle, with O directly below A at distance r.

Find: (1.1) the instantaneous centre of bar 2; (1.2) v_B; (1.3) ω_2; (1.4) a_A (magnitude); (1.5) ω̇_2.
given: Bar 2 = AB = 250 mm. End A is the centre of roller 1, which runs in a circular guide of radius r = 200 mm centred at O. End B is roller 3, which runs along a horizontal guide. Angle α = 25° between bar AB and the horizontal. At the instant: roller-1 centre A has a constant horizontal velocity V_A = 0.8 m/s directed to the LEFT.

steps.title: Set coordinates
steps.content: Place O at the origin. Since OA⊥V_A is vertical, $A=(0,\,r)=(0,\,0.200)\,\text{m}$. Bar to the upper-left: $\vec r_{AB}=AB(-\cos\alpha,\,\sin\alpha)=0.25(-0.906,\,0.423)=(-0.2266,\,0.1057)\,\text{m}$, so $B=(-0.2266,\,0.3057)\,\text{m}$.

steps.title: 1.1 Instantaneous centre
steps.content: $V_A$ is horizontal $\Rightarrow$ its perpendicular through $A$ is the **vertical** line $x=0$. $B$ slides on a horizontal guide so $v_B$ is horizontal $\Rightarrow$ its perpendicular through $B$ is the **vertical** line $x=-0.2266$. The two perpendiculars are parallel: the **ICR is at infinity (vertical direction)** — bar 2 is in instantaneous pure translation.

steps.title: 1.2 & 1.3 Velocity of B and ω₂
steps.content: Pure translation $\Rightarrow \boxed{\omega_2=0}$ and every point has the same velocity: $\vec v_B=\vec v_A=0.8\,\text{m/s}$ to the **left**. (Check with $\vec v_B=\vec v_A+\omega_2\hat k\times\vec r_{AB}$: the $y$-equation gives $0=\omega_2 r_{AB,x}$, and since $r_{AB,x}\neq0$, $\omega_2=0$.)

steps.title: 1.4 Acceleration of A
steps.content: A moves on the circle of radius $r$ with constant speed $V_A$ (given constant). Tangential acceleration $=\dot V_A=0$; only centripetal remains, directed from A toward O (downward): $a_A=\dfrac{V_A^2}{r}=\dfrac{0.8^2}{0.200}=3.2\,\text{m/s}^2$, pointing down.

steps.title: 1.5 Angular acceleration of bar 2
steps.content: Apply $\vec a_B=\vec a_A+\vec\alpha_2\times\vec r_{AB}-\omega_2^2\vec r_{AB}$ with $\omega_2=0$, $\vec a_A=(0,-3.2)$, and the constraint $a_{By}=0$ (B on horizontal guide). The $y$-component: $0=a_{A,y}+\alpha_2\,r_{AB,x}\Rightarrow \alpha_2=-\dfrac{a_{A,y}}{r_{AB,x}}=-\dfrac{-3.2}{-0.2266}=-14.1\,\text{rad/s}^2$. Magnitude $\dot\omega_2=14.1\,\text{rad/s}^2$, sense **clockwise** (negative $\hat k$).

finalAnswer: ICR of bar 2: at infinity (vertical direction) → bar 2 is in instantaneous pure translation. v_B = 0.8 m/s, horizontal, to the left. ω₂ = 0. a_A = 3.2 m/s² (directed toward O, i.e. vertically down). ω̇₂ ≈ 14.1 rad/s², clockwise.
tips: The whole problem hinges on recognising that the given horizontal V_A forces OA to be vertical (A at the top of the circle). With both A and B moving horizontally, the ICR is at infinity and ω₂=0 — a classic 'translation instant'. Don't forget a_A is purely centripetal because |V_A| is constant. For 1.5, the only inertia-free way is the acceleration equation with the guide constraint a_By=0; the sign tells you the sense. If your figure shows A to the right of O at the same height, the same method applies — just re-read the coordinates of A from where OA⊥V_A puts it.

title: Exam Simulation 1 — Problem 2 (vibrations)
meta: Single-DoF free damped oscillations of a pivoted bar
statement:
Geometry from the figure: the bar is horizontal, pinned at the left end O. At the **midpoint A (distance L/2 from O)** two vertical springs k₁ and k₂ connect the bar upward to the fixed ceiling (they act in parallel at the same point). At the **right end B (distance L from O)** a vertical damper β connects the bar to the ground. The generalized coordinate is the small rotation ϑ about O.

Find: (2.1) FBD with dynamic terms; (2.2) k_eq of the two springs; (2.3) equation of motion in canonic form; (2.4) ω_n; (2.5) ω_d.
given: Bar OB: length L, uniform mass m = 12 kg, pivoted at O. Two springs k₁ = 10 kN/m and k₂ = 15 kN/m and one damper β = 100 N·s/m connect it to the frame. Horizontal at static equilibrium; small free oscillations ϑ. I_G = mL²/12.

steps.title: 2.1 Free-body / dynamic terms
steps.content: About O the bar has an inertia moment $I_O\ddot\vartheta$ (restoring-opposing). The springs at $L/2$ give a restoring moment, the damper at $L$ a dissipative moment. Inertia about the pivot: $I_O=I_G+m(L/2)^2=\tfrac{1}{12}mL^2+\tfrac14 mL^2=\tfrac{1}{3}mL^2$.

steps.title: 2.2 Equivalent spring stiffness
steps.content: Both springs attach at the **same point** (midpoint) and both connect bar-to-frame, so they act in **parallel**: $k_{eq}=k_1+k_2=10+15=25\,\text{kN/m}$.

steps.title: 2.3 Equation of motion (canonic form)
steps.content:
Moment balance about O for small ϑ (vertical displacement of a point at distance d is $d\vartheta$, its velocity $d\dot\vartheta$):
$$I_O\ddot\vartheta+\beta\Big(L\Big)^2\dot\vartheta+k_{eq}\Big(\tfrac{L}{2}\Big)^2\vartheta=0.$$
With $I_O=\tfrac13 mL^2$, divide through — **L cancels everywhere**:
$$\ddot\vartheta+\frac{3\beta}{m}\dot\vartheta+\frac{3k_{eq}}{4m}\vartheta=0\;\Rightarrow\;\ddot\vartheta+2\zeta\omega_n\dot\vartheta+\omega_n^2\vartheta=0.

steps.title: 2.4 Natural angular frequency
steps.content: $\omega_n^2=\dfrac{k_{eq}(L/2)^2}{I_O}=\dfrac{3k_{eq}}{4m}=\dfrac{3(25000)}{4(12)}=1562.5\,\text{s}^{-2}\Rightarrow \omega_n=39.5\,\text{rad/s}.$

steps.title: 2.5 Damping ratio and damped frequency
steps.content: $2\zeta\omega_n=\dfrac{\beta L^2}{I_O}=\dfrac{3\beta}{m}=\dfrac{3(100)}{12}=25\,\text{s}^{-1}\Rightarrow \zeta=\dfrac{25}{2(39.53)}=0.316.$ Then $\omega_d=\omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}=39.53\sqrt{1-0.316^2}=37.5\,\text{rad/s}.$

finalAnswer: k_eq = k₁+k₂ = 25 kN/m. Canonic EOM: ϑ̈ + (3β/m)ϑ̇ + (3k_eq/4m)ϑ = 0 → ϑ̈ + 25·ϑ̇ + 1562.5·ϑ = 0. ω_n = √1562.5 ≈ 39.5 rad/s. ζ ≈ 0.316 (underdamped). ω_d = ω_n√(1−ζ²) ≈ 37.5 rad/s.
tips: Key reduction: every element's contribution is (coefficient)×(distance from pivot)². Springs at L/2 → k_eq(L/2)²; damper at L → β·L². Because the bar inertia is I_O=⅓mL², the length L cancels completely, so you never need its value. Watch the parallel-spring rule: same attachment point + same two bodies ⇒ add stiffnesses. The numbers come out clean (ω_n≈39.5, ω_d=37.5), a good sign the setup is right.

title: Exam Simulation 1 — Problem 3 (motion transmission, spur gears)
meta: Four-gear reducer: ratio, torques, tooth force; η = 1
statement:
Geometry from the figure: gear 1 (motor shaft) meshes with gear 2 on the intermediate shaft; gear 3 (same intermediate shaft as 2) meshes with gear 4 on the drum shaft. So power flows 1→2 (shaft I→II) then 3→4 (shaft II→III). The axes of input gear 1 and output gear 4 are collinear, which forces the two centre distances to be equal.

Find: (3.1) z₄ and ratio i = ω₁/ω₄; (3.2) torque diagram (transient); (3.3) motor-side dynamic equation; (3.4) drum resistant torque C_D; (3.5) force exchanged between gears 1 and 2.
given: Motor M — reducer R (four spur gears) — drum D. z₁ = 21, z₂ = 39, z₃ = 25; module m = 5 mm (all gears); pressure angle α = 20°; efficiency η = 1. Axes of gears 1 and 4 are aligned. Gear/inertia of gears negligible; motor and drum inertias I_m, I_D. Steady state: n₁ = 500 rpm = const, P = 5 kW.

steps.title: 3.1 z₄ and transmission ratio
steps.content: Aligned axes 1 & 4 ⇒ equal centre distances: $\tfrac{m}{2}(z_1+z_2)=\tfrac{m}{2}(z_3+z_4)\Rightarrow z_4=z_1+z_2-z_3=21+39-25=35.$ Ratio (input/output): $i=\dfrac{\omega_1}{\omega_4}=\dfrac{z_2}{z_1}\cdot\dfrac{z_4}{z_3}=\dfrac{39}{21}\cdot\dfrac{35}{25}=1.857\times1.4=2.6.$

steps.title: 3.2 Torques in transient
steps.content: Motor M: driving torque $C_m$ minus its inertia torque $I_m\dot\omega_1$; reducer R (massless gears): pure pair of meshing forces, no net stored torque; drum D: resistant $C_D$ plus its inertia torque $I_D\dot\omega_4$. Arrows: $C_m$ drives ω₁; $C_D$ and the inertia couples oppose the acceleration.

steps.title: 3.3 Dynamic equation seen by the motor
steps.content:
Reflect the drum inertia to the motor shaft through $i$ (ω₄=ω₁/i ⇒ $\dot\omega_4=\dot\omega_1/i$, torque scales by $1/i$ with η=1). Equation reduced to the motor axis:
$$C_m-\frac{C_D}{i}=\Big(I_m+\frac{I_D}{i^2}\Big)\dot\omega_1.$$
The term $I_D/i^2$ is the drum inertia reflected to the motor shaft.

steps.title: 3.4 Drum resistant torque (steady state)
steps.content: Steady state $\dot\omega=0$, η = 1 ⇒ power conserved. Motor speed $\omega_1=\dfrac{2\pi n_1}{60}=\dfrac{2\pi(500)}{60}=52.36\,\text{rad/s}$. Motor torque $C_m=P/\omega_1=5000/52.36=95.5\,\text{N·m}$. Drum speed $\omega_4=\omega_1/i=52.36/2.6=20.14\,\text{rad/s}$, so $C_D=P/\omega_4=5000/20.14=248\,\text{N·m}$ (equals $i\,C_m=2.6\times95.5$).

steps.title: 3.5 Force exchanged between gears 1 and 2
steps.content: Pitch radius of gear 1: $r_1=\dfrac{m z_1}{2}=\dfrac{0.005\times21}{2}=0.0525\,\text{m}$. Tangential (transmitted) force: $F_t=\dfrac{C_m}{r_1}=\dfrac{95.5}{0.0525}=1819\,\text{N}$. If the full normal tooth force is wanted: $F_{n}=\dfrac{F_t}{\cos\alpha}=\dfrac{1819}{\cos20^\circ}=1936\,\text{N}$. FBD of gear 1: driving torque $C_m$ (CCW per figure) balanced by the tooth reaction $F_t$ tangent at radius $r_1$, plus the radial component $F_r=F_t\tan20^\circ=662\,\text{N}$ and the bearing reaction at the centre.

finalAnswer: z₄ = 35; i = ω₁/ω₄ = (z₂/z₁)(z₄/z₃) = 2.6. Motor-side equation: C_m − C_D/i = (I_m + I_D/i²)ω̇₁. Steady state: C_m = P/ω₁ = 95.5 N·m, C_D = i·C_m = P/ω₄ ≈ 248 N·m. Tangential exchanged force F₁,₂ = C_m/r₁ ≈ 1.82 kN (normal force F_n = F_t/cos20° ≈ 1.94 kN).
tips: The aligned-axes trick gives z₄ in one line: z₁+z₂=z₃+z₄. With η=1 it is easiest to use power P=Cω: the same 5 kW flows everywhere, so C_D=P/ω₄ directly (and equals i·C_m). For 3.5 the 'exchanged force between gears' on the pitch circle is the tangential transmitted force F_t=C_m/r₁; if the grader wants the actual tooth (normal) force, divide by cos α. Reflected inertia I_D/i² is the standard way the drum inertia appears on the motor shaft.

title: Exam Simulation 2 — Problem 1 (slider–crank kinematics)
meta: Piston-rod–crank: ICR, velocities, accelerations
statement:
Geometry from the figure: O is fixed (origin), the x-axis points right, y up. The **crank OB** (length 100 mm) is vertical at this instant with B directly above O, so $B=(0,\,0.1)\,\text{m}$. The **connecting rod AB** (rod 2, length 300 mm) joins the slider A to the crank pin B. A is a piston/slider (block 3) moving along the **horizontal** axis through O, with given velocity v_A = 12 î m/s (to the right) and acceleration a_A = −312 î m/s². From $|AB|=0.3$ and A on the x-axis: $x_A=-\sqrt{0.3^2-0.1^2}=-0.283$ m, so $A=(-0.283,\,0)$ m.

Find: (1.1) ICR of rod 2 (x_C, y_C); (1.2) v_B; (1.3) ω₁ of crank; (1.4) a_B; (1.5) ω̇₁.
given: v_A = 12 î m/s, a_A = −312 î m/s², θ = 90°; AB = 300 mm, OB = 100 mm. Axis OA horizontal, crank vertical.

steps.title: 1.1 ICR of rod 2
steps.content: A's velocity is horizontal ⇒ perpendicular through A is the vertical line $x=-0.283$. B is on the crank (rotation about O), so $v_B\perp OB$; OB is vertical ⇒ $v_B$ horizontal ⇒ perpendicular through B is the vertical line $x=0$. Two vertical (parallel) lines ⇒ **ICR at infinity**: rod 2 is in instantaneous pure translation, $\omega_2=0$. (x_C, y_C → ∞ along the vertical direction.)

steps.title: 1.2 & 1.3 v_B and ω₁
steps.content: Rod 2 translates ⇒ $\vec v_B=\vec v_A=12\,\hat i$ m/s (to the right). B belongs to the crank: $\vec v_B=\omega_1\hat k\times\vec r_{OB}=\omega_1\hat k\times(0,0.1)=(-0.1\omega_1,\,0)$. Matching: $-0.1\,\omega_1=12\Rightarrow \omega_1=-120\,\text{rad/s}$. Magnitude $\omega_1=120\,\text{rad/s}$, sense **clockwise** (B at top moving right).

steps.title: 1.4 & 1.5 accelerations
steps.content:
Two relations: rod 2: $\vec a_B=\vec a_A+\alpha_2\hat k\times\vec r_{AB}$ (with $\omega_2=0$). Crank: $\vec a_B=\alpha_1\hat k\times\vec r_{OB}-\omega_1^2\vec r_{OB}$. Set them equal — two scalar equations in $\alpha_1,\alpha_2$.
$x:\;a_{A,x}-\alpha_2 r_{AB,y}=-\alpha_1 r_{OB,y}-\omega_1^2 r_{OB,x}$
$y:\;a_{A,y}+\alpha_2 r_{AB,x}=\alpha_1 r_{OB,x}-\omega_1^2 r_{OB,y}$
With $a_A=(-312,0)$, $\omega_1^2=14400$, $r_{OB}=(0,0.1)$, $r_{AB}=(0.283,0.1)$:
Solving gives $\alpha_1=-1971\,\text{rad/s}^2$ and $\alpha_2=-5091\,\text{rad/s}^2$.

finalAnswer: ICR of rod 2: at infinity (vertical direction) — rod 2 in instantaneous pure translation. v_B = 12 m/s, horizontal, to the right (= v_A). ω₁ = 120 rad/s, clockwise. a_B = (197, −1440) m/s², |a_B| ≈ 1.45×10³ m/s². ω̇₁ = α₁ ≈ 1971 rad/s², clockwise.
tips: At θ=90° the crank is vertical, so both A and B move horizontally and the connecting rod is instantaneously translating (ICR at infinity, ω₂=0). That makes v_B=v_A trivially and ω₁ follows from v_B=ω₁·OB. For accelerations you must keep the centripetal term −ω₁²·r_OB on the crank (that's the big −1440 m/s² downward component). The two acceleration equations (rod + crank) solve simultaneously for α₁ and α₂; report |a_B|≈1.45 km/s² and ω̇₁≈1971 rad/s² clockwise. Signs of the senses depend on the î direction shown — keep v_A to the right as given.

title: Exam Simulation 2 — Problem 2 (helical gears)
meta: Transverse quantities, centre distance, torque & tooth-force components
statement:
Geometry from the figure: a helical gear pair, gear 1 (motor side, z₁=30) driving gear 2 (load side, z₂=105). The motor M turns gear 1 at constant speed; a constant resisting torque C_u acts on the gear-2 shaft. The figure shows the tangential T, radial R and axial directions on the tooth.

Find: (2.1) transverse module m_t; (2.2) transverse pitch p_t; (2.3) centre distance a; (2.4) C_u; (2.5) tangential, radial and axial components of the tooth force on gear 1.
given: C_M = 210 N·m (motor torque on gear 1 shaft); z₁ = 30, z₂ = 105; η = 1; normal module m_n = 6 mm; normal pressure angle α_n = 20°; helix angle β = 14°. Constant-speed motor; constant resisting torque C_u on gear 2.

steps.title: 2.1 Transverse (frontal) module
steps.content: $m_t=\dfrac{m_n}{\cos\beta}=\dfrac{6}{\cos14^\circ}=\dfrac{6}{0.9703}=6.18\,\text{mm}.$

steps.title: 2.3 Centre distance
steps.content: Use transverse module: $a=\dfrac{m_t}{2}(z_1+z_2)=\dfrac{6.184}{2}(30+105)=3.092\times135=417.4\,\text{mm}.$

steps.title: 2.4 Resistant torque on gear 2
steps.content: Ratio $i=\dfrac{\omega_1}{\omega_2}=\dfrac{z_2}{z_1}=\dfrac{105}{30}=3.5$. With η = 1 (power conserved): $C_u=i\,C_M=3.5\times210=735\,\text{N·m}.$

steps.title: 2.5 Tooth-force components on gear 1
steps.content: Pitch radius of gear 1: $r_1=\dfrac{m_t z_1}{2}=\dfrac{6.184\times30}{2}=92.76\,\text{mm}$. Tangential: $F_t=\dfrac{C_M}{r_1}=\dfrac{210}{0.09276}=2264\,\text{N}$. Transverse pressure angle: $\tan\alpha_t=\dfrac{\tan\alpha_n}{\cos\beta}\Rightarrow\alpha_t=20.56^\circ$. Radial: $F_r=F_t\tan\alpha_t=2264\times0.3751=849\,\text{N}$. Axial (thrust): $F_a=F_t\tan\beta=2264\times0.2493=564\,\text{N}$. (Resultant normal $F_n=\dfrac{F_t}{\cos\alpha_t\cos\beta}=2492\,\text{N}$.)

finalAnswer: m_t = m_n/cosβ ≈ 6.18 mm. p_t = π m_t ≈ 19.43 mm. a = (m_t/2)(z₁+z₂) ≈ 417.4 mm. i = z₂/z₁ = 3.5 ⇒ C_u = i·C_M = 735 N·m. On gear 1: F_t = C_M/r₁ ≈ 2264 N (tangential, opposing motion), F_R = F_t·tanα_t ≈ 849 N (radial, toward the centre), F_A = F_t·tanβ ≈ 564 N (axial, along the shaft).
tips: Everything geometric uses the TRANSVERSE quantities, not the normal ones: m_t=m_n/cosβ, and α_t from tanα_t=tanα_n/cosβ (α_t≈20.56°, slightly larger than 20°). A common slip is computing the centre distance with m_n — that underestimates a. C_u from η=1 is simply i·C_M. Senses: F_t opposes the driven motion of gear 1's tooth, F_R points toward gear 1's centre, and F_A is the axial thrust set by the helix β. If the grader uses the data verbatim, note ω₂=0.2 is labelled 'm/s' on the sheet — it is an angular speed and does not affect any of these results.

title: Exam Simulation 2 — Problem 3 (belt + band brake, braking dynamics)
meta: Belt ratio, capstan band brake, deceleration and stop time
statement:
Geometry from the figure: the load m hangs from a rope wound on drum 1 (r₁) and descends with velocity v. Drum 1 is rigidly joined to belt pulley 2 (r₂). A belt (in adherence) connects pulley 2 to pulley 3 (r₃); pulley 3 is rigidly joined to brake drum 4 (r₄). A band brake wraps drum 4: its band ends (tensions T₃ upper, T₄ lower) attach to a vertical lever pinned at the bottom (point O). The activating force F is applied horizontally at the top of the lever. Vertical lever distances from the pivot O: F at height a = 1.2 m, the upper band end (T₃) at b = 0.9 m, the lower band end (T₄) at c = 0.3 m.

Find: (3.1) belt ratio i = ω₃/ω₂; (3.2) FBDs; (3.3) deceleration ẍ of m; (3.4) time to stop.
given: m = 100 kg; F = 700 N (brake force); r₁ = 0.2 m; r₂ = 0.4 m, r₃ = 0.2 m; r₄ = 0.3 m; a = 1.2 m, b = 0.9 m, c = 0.3 m; f = 0.2 (band–drum friction); (ω₄)₀ = 10 rad/s at brake activation. All bodies except m massless/inertialess.

steps.title: 3.1 Belt transmission ratio
steps.content: Belt in adherence ⇒ equal belt speed: $\omega_2 r_2=\omega_3 r_3\Rightarrow i=\dfrac{\omega_3}{\omega_2}=\dfrac{r_2}{r_3}=\dfrac{0.4}{0.2}=2.$

steps.title: Kinematic chain & initial speed
steps.content: Drum1–pulley2 rigid ⇒ ω₁=ω₂. Pulley3–drum4 rigid ⇒ ω₄=ω₃=2ω₂. Load: $v=\omega_1 r_1=\omega_2 r_1$. At activation ω₄=10 ⇒ ω₂=ω₄/i=5 rad/s ⇒ $v_0=\omega_2 r_1=5(0.2)=1\,\text{m/s}$, and $\ddot x=\dot\omega_2 r_1$.

steps.title: 3.2 Band brake — tensions (capstan)
steps.content: Wrap angle ≈ 180° = π. Capstan: $\dfrac{T_{tight}}{T_{slack}}=e^{f\pi}=e^{0.2\pi}=1.874$. Lever moment about O (band ends pull the lever, F balances): $F\,a=T_3\,b+T_4\,c$ with $T_3=e^{f\pi}T_4$. Solve: $T_4=\dfrac{F a}{e^{f\pi}b+c}=\dfrac{700(1.2)}{1.874(0.9)+0.3}=\dfrac{840}{1.987}=423\,\text{N}$, $T_3=792\,\text{N}$. Brake torque on drum 4: $M_4=(T_3-T_4)r_4=(792-423)(0.3)=110.9\,\text{N·m}.$

steps.title: Reflect brake torque to drum 1 (massless bodies)
steps.content: All bodies massless ⇒ torque balance on each. Pulley3–drum4: belt transmitted force $F_b=\dfrac{M_4}{r_3}=\dfrac{110.9}{0.2}=554\,\text{N}$. Drum1–pulley2: rope tension $T_{rope}=\dfrac{F_b r_2}{r_1}=\dfrac{554(0.4)}{0.2}=1109\,\text{N}.$ (Equivalently $T_{rope}=M_4\,r_2/(r_3 r_1)$.)

steps.title: 3.3 Deceleration of the load
steps.content: Newton on the descending mass (down positive): $m\ddot x=mg-T_{rope}=100(9.81)-1109=981-1109=-128\,\text{N}\Rightarrow \ddot x=-1.28\,\text{m/s}^2.$ The negative sign = deceleration (the brake makes the rope tension exceed the weight).

steps.title: 3.4 Time to stop
steps.content: Constant deceleration from $v_0=1\,\text{m/s}$: $t=\dfrac{v_0}{|\ddot x|}=\dfrac{1}{1.28}=0.78\,\text{s}.$

finalAnswer: i = ω₃/ω₂ = r₂/r₃ = 2. Initial load speed v₀ = ω₄r₁/i = 1 m/s. Band-brake (wrap π): T₃ ≈ 792 N, T₄ ≈ 423 N, brake torque M₄ ≈ 111 N·m → rope tension ≈ 1109 N. Deceleration ẍ ≈ −1.28 m/s² (the mass slows). Stop time t = v₀/|ẍ| ≈ 0.78 s.
tips: UNCERTAINTY FLAG: the band-brake lever geometry and the wrap angle are not fully legible from the figure, so two modelling choices affect the numbers: (i) wrap angle — I took 180° (e^{fπ}=1.874); (ii) the lever moment equation — I used F·a = T₃·b + T₄·c (both band ends pull the lever the same way). With those, ẍ≈−1.28 m/s² and t≈0.78 s. If the brake is the differential type (F·a = T₃·b − T₄·c), the same method gives a larger torque (M₄≈159 N·m, T_rope≈1589 N, ẍ≈−6.1 m/s², t≈0.16 s). The robust parts are i=2, v₀=1 m/s, and the reflection chain T_rope = M₄·r₂/(r₃r₁); pick the lever equation matching your figure and the capstan ratio e^{fθ} for the actual wrap θ. Sanity check: braking requires T_rope>mg=981 N, which both cases satisfy.
